```text
              (now multiply back up)
   = 4 * (3 * (2 * (1 * 1))) = 24
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Explain the systematic approach to designing a program in a functional programming language. List any four steps.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The systematic approach to designing a functional program involves the following steps:
1. **Problem Analysis** — Identify inputs, outputs, and the precise relationship between them.
2. **Type Signature Definition** — Declare the function's type, which constrains the design space.
3. **Example-Based Specification** — Provide at least 3 test cases defining expected behaviour.
4. **Generalization via Recursion** — Identify the base case and recursive case, then express the function as a recursive equation.
5. **Refinement** — Replace explicit recursion with higher-order functions like `map`, `foldr`, or `filter` wherever possible.
6. **Verification** — Test in GHCi and validate against the examples.

*(Any four well-explained steps fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Compare top-down and bottom-up program design approaches with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Top-Down | Bottom-Up |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Goal → Sub-problems | Primitives → Goal |
| **FP Suitability** | Moderate | **Highly idiomatic** |
| **Example** | Start with `compiler`, then derive `lexer`, `parser` | Start with `map`, `foldr`, then compose `compiler = codegen . parser . lexer` |

In functional programming, **bottom-up design is preferred** because it mirrors mathematical construction of complex functions from simpler ones, and exploits the rich type system of Haskell.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **six-step methodology** for designing a functional program. Illustrate each step with the design of a function `lengthList :: [a] -> Int` that returns the number of elements in a list.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the same methodology, design a function `reverseList :: [a] -> [a]` that reverses a list. Show the complete Haskell code, the type signature, and a trace of `reverseList [1,2,3]`.

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Step 1 — Specification:** Input is a polymorphic list `[a]`; output is the count of its elements as an `Int`.

**Step 2 — Type Signature:**
```haskell
lengthList :: [a] -> Int
```

**Step 3 — Test Cases:**
```haskell
lengthList []        = 0
lengthList [42]      = 1
lengthList [1,2,3,4] = 4
```

**Step 4 — Base and Recursive Cases:**
- Base: empty list returns 0.
- Recursive: `(x:xs)` returns `1 + lengthList xs`.

**Step 5 — Haskell Code:**
```haskell
lengthList :: [a] -> Int
lengthList []     = 0
lengthList (x:xs) = 1 + lengthList xs
```

**Step 6 — GHCi Verification:**
```haskell
ghci> lengthList [1,2,3,4]
4
ghci> lengthList []
0
```

**Generalized form:**
```haskell
lengthList = foldr (\_ acc -> acc + 1) 0
```

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Specification:** Reverse the order of elements in a list.

**Type Signature:**
```haskell
reverseList :: [a] -> [a]
```

**Test Cases:**
```haskell
reverseList []        = []
reverseList [1]       = [1]
reverseList [1,2,3]   = [3,2,1]
```

**Recursive Insight:** The reverse of `(x:xs)` is `reverseList xs` followed by `[x]`, i.e., `reverseList xs ++ [x]`.

**Haskell Code:**
```haskell
reverseList :: [a] -> [a]
reverseList []     = []
reverseList (x:xs) = reverseList xs ++ [x]
```

**Trace of `reverseList [1,2,3]`:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{reverseList}\,[1,2,3] &= \text{reverseList}\,[2,3] \,\,+\!\!+ \,\, [1] \\
&= (\text{reverseList}\,[3] \,\,+\!\!+ \,\, [2]) \,\,+\!\!+ \,\, [1] \\
&= ((\text{reverseList}\,[] \,\,+\!\!+ \,\, [3]) \,\,+\!\!+ \,\, [2]) \,\,+\!\!+ \,\, [1] \\
&= (([\,] \,\,+\!\!+ \,\, [3]) \,\,+\!\!+ \,\, [2]) \,\,+\!\!+ \,\, [1] \\
&= ([3] \,\,+\!\!+ \,\, [2]) \,\,+\!\!+ \,\, [1] \\
&= [3,2] \,\,+\!\!+ \,\, [1] \\
&= [3,2,1] \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is writing `reverseList (x:xs) = x : reverseList xs` — this is **identity**, not reverse! Students lose 2-3 marks here. The correct operator is `++` (list concatenation) applied to the **tail**, not the head.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Design a Haskell function `myLast :: [a] -> a` that returns the last element of a non-empty list. Show the complete design process and trace the execution for `myLast [10,20,30]`.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **type-driven development** in functional programming. How does the type system of Haskell assist in program design? Provide one illustrative example.

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Specification:** Return the final element of a list (input guaranteed non-empty).

**Type Signature:**
```haskell
myLast :: [a] -> a
```

**Test Cases:**
```haskell
myLast [5]         = 5
myLast [1,2,3,4]   = 4
myLast ['a','b']   = 'b'
```

**Recursive Insight:** For a single-element list `[x]`, return `x`. For a multi-element list `(x:xs)`, recurse on the tail.

**Haskell Code:**
```haskell
myLast :: [a] -> a
myLast [x]    = x
myLast (_:xs) = myLast xs
```

**Trace of `myLast [10,20,30]`:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{myLast}\,[10,20,30] &= \text{myLast}\,[20,30] \\
&= \text{myLast}\,[30] \\
&= 30 \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Defensive Programming Note:** The pattern `[x]` matches lists of *exactly one element*, while `(_:xs)` matches lists of *one or more elements*. Together, they form an exhaustive and non-overlapping pattern set.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Type-Driven Development (TDD-FP):**
Type-driven development is the practice of **writing the type signature first** and using it as a *design contract* that guides the implementation. In Haskell, the **Hindley–Milner type inference system** (with extensions) offers several design advantages:

1. **Compile-Time Safety:** Many runtime errors in other languages (e.g., null pointer exceptions, type mismatches) are caught at compile time.
2. **Self-Documenting Code:** A type signature like `parseInt :: String -> Maybe Int` immediately tells the reader what the function does and its failure mode.
3. **Constrains the Solution Space:** The compiler-generated types often leave only one or two plausible implementations.
4. **Polymorphism Encourages Reuse:** Writing `length :: [a] -> Int` once lets the function work on lists of *any* type.

**Illustrative Example:**
```haskell
-- Type signature first
squareSum :: Num a => [a] -> a
squareSum []     = 0
squareSum (x:xs) = x*x + squareSum xs
```
Here, the type `Num a => [a] -> a` tells us:
- The list elements must support numeric operations (`Num`).
- The function works for `Int`, `Integer`, `Float`, `Double`, etc.
- The result type matches the element type.

The compiler **guides** the programmer — if a student accidentally writes `squareSum (x:xs) = x + squareSum xs`, GHC will infer a more general type and the test cases will fail, prompting correction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):** Students often **omit the type class constraint** (e.g., `Num a =>`) and write `squareSum :: [a] -> a`. This is a **type error** because the function uses `*` and `+`, which are not defined on all types. Always include necessary constraints — losing **1 mark** if missed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Designing and Writing Programs**

- ✅ **Functional programming is declarative:** you describe the *what*, not the *how*.
- ✅ **Always write the type signature FIRST** — it is your design contract.
- ✅ The **six-step methodology** is: Specify → Type → Examples → Recursion → Refine → Test.
- ✅ **Base case** is the smallest input (e.g., `[]` for lists, `0` for naturals).
- ✅ **Recursive case** must make progress toward the base case.
- ✅ **Bottom-up design** is preferred in FP; **top-down** is for architecture.
- ✅ **Higher-order functions** (`map`, `filter`, `foldr`, `foldl`) eliminate explicit recursion.
- ✅ The identity element of `+` is `0`; the identity element of `*` is `1`.
- ✅ Use `++` for list concatenation; use `:` (cons) for prepending a single element.
- ✅ Pattern matching must be **exhaustive** — cover all constructor cases.
- ✅ Polymorphic types use type variables `a`, `b`, `c`; type classes constrain them (`Eq a`, `Num a`, `Ord a`).
- ✅ **Trace recursion explicitly** in exams — examiners award partial marks for correct traces even if final code has minor errors.
- ✅ **Equational reasoning** is the FP debugging tool: substitute definitions step by step.
- ✅ **KTU-favourite question pattern:** "Design a function to do X. Show type, examples, code, and trace."
- ✅ **Common error:** `reverse (x:xs) = x : reverse xs` is identity, not reversal.
- ✅ **Common error:** Omitting `Num a =>` constraint when using `+` or `*` in polymorphic functions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Types, Tuples and Lists
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST413 - FUNCTIONAL PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Types, Tuples and Lists

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In **Haskell** (the canonical language of the KTU Functional Programming syllabus), every value and every expression is statically associated with a **type**. A *type* is a label that classifies a value into a set of values possessing identical structural and operational behavior. The collection of supported primitive types, together with the rules for composing compound types, is called the **Haskell Type System**, and it is **static**, **strong**, and **type-inferred**.

### 1.1 The Three Families of Types We Cover in Module 1

| Family | Examples | KTU One-Line Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Basic Scalar Types** | `Int`, `Integer`, `Float`, `Double`, `Bool`, `Char` | Atomic, non-decomposable values. |
| **Tuples** | `(Int, Bool)`, `(Char, [Int])` | Fixed-length, **heterogeneous**, ordered aggregates. |
| **Lists** | `[Int]`, `[Char]`, `[[Int]]` | Variable-length, **homogeneous**, ordered sequences. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 explicitly requires mastery of *basic types, tuples, and lists*, including polymorphism, pattern matching on them, and list comprehensions. These are the **building blocks** for every higher-order function and algebraic data type you will see later.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **type** as a *sticker* that the Haskell compiler (GHC) glues onto every value at compile-time:

- A **basic type** like `Int` is like a single fruit — one banana, one mango. You cannot mix them under the same sticker.
- A **tuple** is a *gift box with a fixed number of labeled slots*. A `(String, Int)` is a two-slot box: left slot must hold a name, right slot must hold a number. Boxes of different slot counts are **different types**.
- A **list** is a *chain of identical boxes*. Every box in the chain must hold the **same kind of sticker**. You may add or remove boxes at any time — the chain length is dynamic, but its element type is fixed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does Haskell care so much about types?** Because types are not just comments — they are a **logic system**. If a program compiles, GHC has mathematically proven that well-typed expressions cannot go wrong at runtime (a property known as **type soundness**). This is the strongest guarantee a programming language can give you.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants Worth Memorizing

| Constant / Metric | Value / Range | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `maxBound :: Int` | **2⁶³ − 1** | Upper limit of fixed-precision integers (64-bit GHC). |
| `minBound :: Int` | **−2⁶³** | Lower limit of fixed-precision integers. |
| `length` of a list | Cardinality $\vert L \vert$ | Number of elements — must be finite in Haskell. |
| String `= [Char]` | Unification rule | A `String` is **syntactic sugar** for a list of characters. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic of Haskell's Type Inference (Hindley–Milner)

When you write `let x = 5 + 3`, you never explicitly say *what type `x` is*. Haskell's **type inference engine** (Hindley–Milner algorithm, $\mathcal{W}$) performs the following logical steps:

1. **Literal typing:** The literal `5` is tentatively assigned type `Num a => a`. The literal `3` is similarly `Num a => a`.
2. **Operator constraint:** `+` has type `Num a => a -> a -> a`. Both operands must share the same numeric type.
3. **Unification:** GHC unifies the two unknown `a` variables. Since the literals are integers, it resolves them to `Integer` (default) or `Int` (monomorphic contexts).
4. **Binding:** The name `x` is then assigned the inferred type, and `x :: Integer` is logged into the inferred-type database.

> **Why this matters for KTU:** Type-inference questions are a **high-frequency Part A topic** ("What is the type of this expression?"). You must read expressions top-down and apply unification mentally.

### 2.2 Tuples — The Algebra of Compound Values

A tuple $T$ of arity $n$ over types $\tau_1, \tau_2, \dots, \tau_n$ is written $(\tau_1, \tau_2, \dots, \tau_n)$. The structural rules are:

- **Arity is part of the type.** `(Int, Bool)` and `(Int, Bool, Char)` are entirely different types.
- **Order is significant.** `(Int, Bool)` is **not** the same as `(Bool, Int)`.
- **Length is fixed at the type level** — you cannot have a tuple of variable length; that is precisely what lists are for.
- **Selectors:** `fst` projects the first slot; `snd` projects the second slot. For higher arities, pattern matching is mandatory (there is no built-in `thd`).

### 2.3 Lists — The Algebra of Recursive Sequences

A list of elements of type $\tau$ is written $[\tau]$. The formal recursive grammar is:

$$\begin{aligned}
[\tau] &\;:=\; [] \;\mid\; \tau : [\tau]
\end{aligned}$$

That is: **a list is either the empty list `[]`, or an element of type $\tau$ *consed* onto another list of type $[\tau]$**. The operator `(:)` — pronounced *cons* — is the single most important list constructor in the entire syllabus.

### 2.4 Polymorphism — The "Any Type" Variable

Haskell types may contain **type variables**, written in lowercase (`a`, `b`, `c`). A function whose type signature contains such variables is **polymorphic**. For example:

$$\text{length} \;::\; [a] \to \text{Int}$$

The `a` here is "any type whatsoever". This is the foundation of code reuse in Haskell: **one `length` function works for lists of integers, lists of strings, lists of tuples — anything.**

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is your **single-source revision artifact** for Module 1 derivations. Memorize both signatures and semantics.

| Function / Operator | Type Signature | Semantics |
|---|---|---|
| `(:)` | $a \to [a] \to [a]$ | Prepend element (cons) |
| `(++)` | $[a] \to [a] \to [a]$ | Concatenate two lists |
| `head` | $[a] \to a$ | First element (partial on `[]`) |
| `tail` | $[a] \to [a]$ | List without first element (partial on `[]`) |
| `null` | $[a] \to \text{Bool}$ | `True` iff list is empty |
| `length` | $[a] \to \text{Int}$ | Cardinality $\vert L \vert$ |
| `last` | $[a] \to a$ | Last element (partial on `[]`) |
| `init` | $[a] \to [a]$ | List without last element |
| `reverse` | $[a] \to [a]$ | Reverse the order |
| `take` | $\text{Int} \to [a] \to [a]$ | First $n$ elements |
| `drop` | $\text{Int} \to [a] \to [a]$ | Discard first $n$ elements |
| `fst` | $(a,b) \to a$ | First projection |
| `snd` | $(a,b) \to b$ | Second projection |
| `zip` | $[a] \to [b] \to [(a,b)]$ | Pair-wise merge, truncates to shorter |
| `unzip` | $[(a,b)] \to ([a],[b])$ | Inverse of `zip` |
| `elem` | $\text{Eq } a \Rightarrow a \to [a] \to \text{Bool}$ | Membership test |
| `map` | $(a \to b) \to [a] \to [b]$ | Apply function to each element |
| `filter` | $(a \to \text{Bool}) \to [a] \to [a]$ | Keep elements satisfying predicate |

> **Real-world engineering utility:** In compiler construction, lists of tokens are threaded through every phase of the front-end. Tuples model AST nodes `(Token, Lexeme)`. In financial systems, tuples represent `(Ticker, Price, Timestamp)` market ticks. The polymorphic operations you learn here scale directly to industrial Haskell codebases at companies like Standard Chartered, Meta, and Galois Inc.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Type Inference of `fst (head [(1, 'a'), (2, 'b')])`

We work **outside-in**, applying unification at each subexpression.

**Step 1: Literal typing.**
The integer `1` is assigned type `Num a => a`. By default resolution, this becomes `Integer`. Similarly, `2 :: Integer`. The character `'a' :: Char`, `'b' :: Char`.

**Step 2: Tuple construction.**
Each pair `(1,'a')` and `(2,'b')` has its components unified: left slot is `Integer`, right slot is `Char`. Therefore each pair has type `(Integer, Char)`.

**Step 3: List typing.**
The list contains two elements, both of type `(Integer, Char)`. Therefore the list has type `[(Integer, Char)]`. By syntactic sugar, we may also write this as `[ (Integer, Char) ]`.

**Step 4: Applying `head`.**
The signature of `head` is $[a] \to a$. With $a$ unified to `(Integer, Char)`, the result of `head [(1,'a'), (2,'b')]` is `(Integer, Char)`.

**Step 5: Applying `fst`.**
The signature of `fst` is $(a,b) \to a$. The compiler unifies $(a,b)$ with `(Integer, Char)`, binding $a = \text{Integer}$, $b = \text{Char}$. The output is `Integer`.

**Final compiled type:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{fst (head [(1, 'a'), (2, 'b')])} &\;::\; \text{Integer}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Algebraic Proof that `length (xs ++ ys) = length xs + length ys`

Let $L$ be a list. We define length recursively by the equations:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{length}\,[] &\;=\; 0 \\
\text{length}\,(x : L) &\;=\; 1 + \text{length}\,L
\end{aligned}$$

Concatenation is defined by:

$$\begin{aligned}
[] \mathbin{+\!+} ys &\;=\; ys \\
(x : xs) \mathbin{+\!+} ys &\;=\; x : (xs \mathbin{+\!+} ys)
\end{aligned}$$

We prove $\text{length}(xs \mathbin{+\!+} ys) = \text{length}\,xs + \text{length}\,ys$ by **structural induction** on $xs$.

**Base case:** $xs = []$.
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{length}([] \mathbin{+\!+} ys) &\stackrel{\text{(def ++)}}{=} \text{length}\,ys \\
&\stackrel{\text{(assum.)}}{=} 0 + \text{length}\,ys \\
&\stackrel{\text{(def length)}}{=} \text{length}\,[] + \text{length}\,ys \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

**Inductive case:** $xs = (x : xs')$, with induction hypothesis $\text{length}(xs' \mathbin{+\!+} ys) = \text{length}\,xs' + \text{length}\,ys$.
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{length}((x:xs') \mathbin{+\!+} ys) &\stackrel{\text{(def ++)}}{=} \text{length}(x : (xs' \mathbin{+\!+} ys)) \\
&\stackrel{\text{(def length)}}{=} 1 + \text{length}(xs' \mathbin{+\!+} ys) \\
&\stackrel{\text{(IH)}}{=} 1 + \text{length}\,xs' + \text{length}\,ys \\
&\stackrel{\text{(def length)}}{=} \text{length}(x:xs') + \text{length}\,ys \\
&\stackrel{\text{(substitute xs)}}{=} \text{length}\,xs + \text{length}\,ys \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

Q.E.D.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — List Comprehension Evaluation

A list comprehension $[e \mid x \leftarrow xs, p(x)]$ is defined as:

$$\begin{aligned}
[e \mid x \leftarrow []] &\;=\; [] \\
[e \mid x \leftarrow (y:ys), p(x)] &\;=\; [e[x:=y] \mid x \leftarrow ys, p(x)], \quad \text{if } p(y) \\
[e \mid x \leftarrow (y:ys), p(x)] &\;=\; [e \mid x \leftarrow ys, p(x)], \quad \text{if } \neg p(y)
\end{aligned}$$

**Worked example:** Evaluate $[x \cdot x \mid x \leftarrow [1,2,3,4,5], \text{odd}(x)]$.

| Step | Current $x$ | $\text{odd}(x)$? | Output so far |
|---|---|---|---|
| Init | — | — | `[]` |
| 1 | 1 | True | `[1]` |
| 2 | 2 | False | `[1]` |
| 3 | 3 | True | `[1,9]` |
| 4 | 4 | False | `[1,9]` |
| 5 | 5 | True | `[1,9,25]` |
| End | — | — | `[1,9,25]` |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Type of Common KTU Expressions

| Expression | Stepwise Inference | Final Type |
|---|---|---|
| `[(1,'a'),(2,'b')]` | `(1,'a') :: (Integer, Char)`; list unifies | `[(Integer, Char)]` |
| `[(+1), (*2), (^3)]` | Each section is `Num a => a -> a` | `[Num a => a -> a]` |
| `[(1,2),(3,4,5)]` | Heterogeneous arity in list | **Type error** |
| `[fst,snd]` | Both are projections of pairs | `[(a,b) -> a]` (after unification, both reduce to first projection) — actually type-erroneous unless context-typed |
| `length :: [a] -> Int` | Polymorphic, returns fixed `Int` | `[a] -> Int` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Haskell Type-Resolution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    src["Haskell Source Code"] --> lex["Lexer / Parser"]
    lex --> ast["Abstract Syntax Tree"]
    ast --> ren["Renamer"]
    ren --> tc["Type Checker - Hindley Milner W"]
    tc --> unify{"Unification succeeds?"}
    unify -- Yes --> core["Typed Core - STG"]
    unify -- No --> err["Compile Time Type Error"]
    core --> codegen["CodeGen - STG to C or ASM"]
    codegen --> exe["Native Executable"]
```

### 4.2 Memory Layout Schematic: A Haskell List `[1, 2, 3]`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cell1["Cons Cell 1 | head: 1 | tail: ptr -->"]
    cell2["Cons Cell 2 | head: 2 | tail: ptr -->"]
    cell3["Cons Cell 3 | head: 3 | tail: ptr -->"]
    nilnode["Nil Cell - sentinel - data constructor []"]
    listvar["listVar - pointer to cell1"]
    listvar --> cell1
    cell1 --> cell2
    cell2 --> cell3
    cell3 --> nilnode
```

> **Explanation:** Each `(:)` allocates a **cons cell** containing one value and one pointer. The list is terminated by the unique `[]` constructor. This is why `head` and `tail` are $O(1)$ but `last` and `++` are $O(n)$ — there is no backward pointer.

### 4.3 Conceptual Type Lattice for Module 1

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    anyt["Type Universe - all types"]
    scalar["Scalar Types: Int, Integer, Float, Double, Bool, Char"]
    compound["Compound Types"]
    anyt --> scalar
    anyt --> compound
    compound --> tup["Tuple Types - arity n, fixed"]
    compound --> lst["List Types - arity 1, recursive"]
    tup --> tup2["Pair a,b"]
    tup --> tup3["Triple a,b,c"]
    lst --> str["String = Char list"]
    lst --> lstlst["List of Lists = a list b"]
```

### 4.4 Pattern-Matching Flow on a Tuple `(x, y)`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inp["Input: pairVal of type a,b"]
    inp --> m1{"Match against pattern x,y?"}
    m1 -- Yes - x bound to first slot --> use1["Use x as type a in body"]
    m1 -- Yes - y bound to second slot --> use2["Use y as type b in body"]
    m1 -- No --> fail["Runtime incomplete pattern error"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. Confusing `String` with `[Char]` vs `[[Char]]`. A string of strings is `[[Char]]`, **not** `[Char]`.
> 2. Writing `head []` or `tail []` — these throw a **runtime exception** because the patterns are non-exhaustive. Always handle the empty case explicitly.
> 3. Believing `(a,b,c)` has a built-in `fst`/`snd` — it does **not**. You must use pattern matching.
> 4. Forgetting that `length` returns `Int`, not the polymorphic type of the list. This causes unification failures in arithmetic.
> 5. Treating `[1,2,3] = [Int]` syntactically — the actual type is `[Integer]` under defaulting.

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Apr 2024]` What is the difference between a tuple and a list in Haskell? Illustrate with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *tuple* is a finite, **heterogeneous** ordered aggregate whose length is encoded in its type. Example: `(1, "KTU", True) :: (Int, String, Bool)`. A *list* is a homogeneous sequence whose length is dynamic and unknown at the type level. Example: `[1,2,3] :: [Int]`. Key distinctions: (i) tuples hold values of **different** types; lists hold values of the **same** type; (ii) tuples have **fixed** arity; lists have **variable** arity; (iii) every different-length tuple is a **distinct** type, but all lists of `Int` share the single type `[Int]`.

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` What does it mean for a Haskell function to be polymorphic? Give the type signature of `length` as an example and explain each component.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A function is *polymorphic* if its type signature contains one or more lowercase **type variables**, allowing it to operate uniformly over any concrete instantiation. The signature `length :: [a] -> Int` declares: (i) the input is a list whose elements are of *some arbitrary* type `a`; (ii) the output is a fixed-precision `Int`. Because `a` is universally quantified, `length` works on `[Int]`, `[Char]`, `[(Bool,Float)]`, etc., without code duplication.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Define the basic data types available in Haskell. Show, with examples, how the compiler infers types for the expressions `5 :: Int`, `True :: Bool`, and `'Z' :: Char`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the recursive definition of `length` and the `(++)` operator, prove by structural induction that $\text{length}(xs \mathbin{+\!+} ys) = \text{length}\,xs + \text{length}\,ys$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Basic Haskell data types are partitioned into numeric, logical, and character categories:
- **Numeric:** `Int` (fixed-precision, machine-word-sized), `Integer` (arbitrary-precision), `Float` (single-precision IEEE-754), `Double` (double-precision IEEE-754).
- **Logical:** `Bool` with constructors `True`, `False`.
- **Character:** `Char`, a Unicode code point.

For type inference of the given literals:
- The literal `5` is recognized as an integer literal. Without an explicit annotation, GHC's defaulting rule (when in a `Num`-class context) resolves it to `Integer`. The signature `5 :: Int` overrides this with an explicit type ascription.
- The literal `True` is a nullary constructor of the algebraic data type `Bool`. GHC binds it directly to `Bool`.
- The literal `'Z'` is a character literal delimited by single quotes; it is bound to `Char`.

**(b)** Proof by structural induction on `xs` as derived in **§3.2** above. Full credit requires stating the base case, the inductive hypothesis, and the inductive step, each shown with explicit application of the defining equations. (Students following the derivation in §3.2 will receive full marks.)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the syntactic sugar `[a]` for a list type. Show that the list `[1,2,3]` is equivalent to `1 : 2 : 3 : []`. Write the recursive definition of `length` and evaluate `length [1,2,3]` step-by-step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are list comprehensions? Write their formal grammar. Evaluate $[x^2 \mid x \leftarrow [1..6], \text{even}(x)]$ step-by-step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The notation `[a]` is Haskell's syntactic sugar for the type `List a`, which is recursively defined as either the empty list `[]` or the cons of an element onto another list. The list `[1,2,3]` desugars as `1 : (2 : (3 : []))`. The recursive definition of `length`:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{length}\,[] &\;=\; 0 \\
\text{length}\,(x : xs) &\;=\; 1 + \text{length}\,xs
\end{aligned}$$

Step-by-step evaluation of `length [1,2,3]`:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{length}\,(1 : 2 : 3 : []) &\;=\; 1 + \text{length}\,(2 : 3 : []) \\
&\;=\; 1 + (1 + \text{length}\,(3 : [])) \\
&\;=\; 1 + (1 + (1 + \text{length}\,[])) \\
&\;=\; 1 + 1 + 1 + 0 \\
&\;=\; 3
\end{aligned}$$

**(b)** A list comprehension is a declarative syntax for constructing lists by **drawing** elements from one or more source lists, optionally **filtering** with predicates, and **transforming** via an output expression. Its grammar is:

$$\begin{aligned}
[\,e \mid q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n\,] \quad \text{where each } q_i &\;\text{is either}\; x \leftarrow xs \;\text{(generator)}\; \text{or}\; p(x) \;\text{(filter)}
\end{aligned}$$

Evaluation of $[x^2 \mid x \leftarrow [1..6], \text{even}(x)]$:

| Iter | $x$ | $\text{even}(x)$? | $x^2$ | Accumulated |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | False | — | `[]` |
| 2 | 2 | True | 4 | `[4]` |
| 3 | 3 | False | — | `[4]` |
| 4 | 4 | True | 16 | `[4,16]` |
| 5 | 5 | False | — | `[4,16]` |
| 6 | 6 | True | 36 | `[4,16,36]` |

Final result: `[4, 16, 36]`.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **type** in Haskell is a static classification of every value; GHC infers it via Hindley–Milner unification.
- **Basic types**: `Int`, `Integer`, `Float`, `Double`, `Bool`, `Char`. `Integer` is unbounded; `Int` is bounded by **2⁶³ − 1**.
- **Tuples** are fixed-arity, heterogeneous, ordered. `(a,b) ≠ (b,a)`; `(a,b) ≠ (a,b,c)`.
- **Lists** are variable-arity, homogeneous, defined recursively as $[]$ or $x : xs$.
- The **cons operator** `(:)` has type $a \to [a] \to [a]$ and is right-associative: `1:2:3:[]` is `1:(2:(3:[]))`.
- A `String` is **syntactic sugar** for `[Char]`. Always remember this conversion.
- **Polymorphism** uses lowercase type variables (`a`, `b`); it is the engine of code reuse.
- **List comprehensions** combine generators, filters, and an output expression.
- **Partial functions** (`head`, `tail`, `last`, `init`) crash on `[]` — guard them explicitly.
- **`length` returns `Int`**, not the polymorphic element type — do not confuse the two in arithmetic contexts.
- **Structural induction** is the canonical proof technique for any recursive list property (e.g., `length(xs ++ ys) = length xs + length ys`).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: I/O in Haskell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST413 - FUNCTIONAL PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** I/O in Haskell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (I/O in Haskell):** In Haskell, Input/Output (I/O) is modeled as a **first-class type constructor** `IO a`, where `a` denotes the type of the value *produced* by the action. An `IO a` value is **not** a result — it is a *description* (or *program*) of an imperative effectful computation that, when executed by the Haskell runtime system (RTS), will perform some side-effecting interaction with the outside world and yield a value of type `a`.

Every Haskell program must have a top-level entry point of type:

```haskell
main :: IO ()
```

The double colon `::` is read as **"has type"**, and `IO ()` denotes an I/O action that produces *no useful return value* (the unit type `()` is analogous to `void` in C/Java).

### The Trinity of Haskell I/O
| Token | Role |
| :--- | :--- |
| `IO a` | Type of an effectful computation yielding a value of type `a` |
| `>>=` (bind) | Sequencing operator that threads a value from one action to the next |
| `do { ... }` | Syntactic sugar making monadic sequencing look imperative |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** Haskell treats I/O as a *value* in the pure functional world — not as a *statement* to be executed eagerly. This is the **single most distinguishing feature** of I/O in a pure functional language.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Order Ticket"

Imagine a Haskell `IO` action as a **kitchen order ticket** in a restaurant:

- The ticket is **just a piece of paper** — it does not cook anything by itself.
- The chef (the **Haskell runtime system / `main`**) reads the ticket *top-to-bottom* and performs the described actions (taking food from the fridge, calling the customer, etc.).
- You (the programmer) write the *sequence* of tasks; the ticket can reference *previous results* — for example, "Use the name written on line 1 to greet the customer on line 2."
- A function with type `String -> IO ()` is like a **template recipe**: given an ingredient (a `String`), it produces a ticket describing what to do with it.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for students:** *IO a = "An INSTRUCTION, not an INSTANCE."* You are constructing instructions; the RTS performs them exactly once.

---

## 1.3 Why I/O Must Be Special in a Pure Language

Pure Haskell forbids any function from changing a hidden global state. Yet real programs *must* read files, write to the network, and print to the screen. The compromise is:

1. **Wrap** effectful actions in the `IO` type so the type system *warns* the programmer.
2. **Sequence** them explicitly using `>>=` (or the `do` block) so the order of effects is unambiguous and referentially transparent *as a whole program*.
3. The **only** way to "escape" `IO` and observe its result is via `main` (or `unsafePerformIO`, which is unsafe and non-standard).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student misconception:** "An `IO a` action *is* an effect when written." **False.** It is *only* an effect when `main` runs it. You can freely construct, pass, and return `IO` values inside pure code without any side-effect happening.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The `IO` Type Constructor — Internal View

`IO` is a *parameterised type* (technically a *type constructor* of kind `* -> *`).

```haskell
data IO a   -- abstract; you cannot write your own
type IO ()  = an I/O action returning "nothing useful"
```

- A value of type `IO Char` is a *program* that, when run, will read a character.
- A value of type `IO ()` is a *program* that will perform some visible side-effect and return nothing.

---

## 2.2 Sequencing I/O Actions

### 2.2.1 The `>>` Operator (Sequence, Discard Result)

$$
\textsf{>>} \;:\; \text{IO } a \;\to\; \text{IO } b \;\to\; \text{IO } b
$$

It executes the left action, *discards* its result, then executes the right action.

```haskell
putStr "Hello" >> putStrLn " World"
-- "Hello World\n" is printed.
```

### 2.2.2 The `>>=` Operator (Bind, Thread Value)

$$
\textsf{>>=} \;:\; \text{IO } a \;\to\; (a \to \text{IO } b) \;\to\; \text{IO } b
$$

It executes the left action, **feeds its result** into the supplied function, and yields the *next* action — which is then executed.

```haskell
getLine >>= \name -> putStrLn ("Hi " ++ name)
```

### 2.2.3 The `do` Notation — Desugaring Rules

| `do` Block | Equivalent Pure Expression |
| :--- | :--- |
| `do { e }` | `e` |
| `do { e1 ; e2 ; ... ; en }` | `e1 >> e2 >> ... >> en` |
| `do { x <- e1 ; e2 }` | `e1 >>= \x -> e2` |
| `do { let x = expr ; e }` | `(\x -> e) expr` (no `IO` wrapper, just `let`) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> `x <- action` is **NOT** assignment. It is **pattern-matching** on the result of executing `action`. The arrow points from the action to the variable, indicating *data flow*.

---

## 2.3 Standard I/O Functions (KTU High-Yield Library)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{getChar}   &\;:\; \text{IO Char} \\
\text{putChar}   &\;:\; \text{Char} \to \text{IO ()} \\
\text{getLine}   &\;:\; \text{IO String} \\
\text{getContents} &\;:\; \text{IO String} \\
\text{putStr}    &\;:\; \text{String} \to \text{IO ()} \\
\text{putStrLn}  &\;:\; \text{String} \to \text{IO ()} \\
\text{print}     &\;:\; \text{Show } a \Rightarrow a \to \text{IO ()} \\
\text{readFile}  &\;:\; \text{FilePath} \to \text{IO String} \\
\text{writeFile} &\;:\; \text{FilePath} \to \text{String} \to \text{IO ()} \\
\text{appendFile} &\;:\; \text{FilePath} \to; \text{String} \to \text{IO ()}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where `FilePath` is just a *type synonym* for `String` (`type FilePath = String`).

### The `print` Function — Desugared

```haskell
print x = putStrLn (show x)
```

It is simply *show* glued to *putStrLn*. Therefore `print (2+2)` prints `"4"`.

---

## 2.4 File Handles — Granular I/O

When you need streaming, partial reads, or precise control, you work with the **`Handle`** type from `System.IO`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{openFile}   &\;:\; \text{FilePath} \to \text{IOMode} \to \text{IO Handle} \\
\text{hClose}     &\;:\; \text{Handle} \to \text{IO ()} \\
\text{hGetChar}   &\;:\; \text{Handle} \to \text{IO Char} \\
\text{hGetLine}   &\;:\; \text{Handle} \to \text{IO String} \\
\text{hGetContents} &\;:\; \text{Handle} \to \text{IO String} \\
\text{hPutStr}    &\;:\; \text{Handle} \to \text{String} \to \text{IO ()} \\
\text{hPutStrLn}  &\;:\; \text{Handle} \to \text{String} \to \text{IO ()} \\
\text{hIsEOF}     &\;:\; \text{Handle} \to \text{IO Bool}
\end{aligned}
$$

### `IOMode` Data Type

```haskell
data IOMode = ReadMode | WriteMode | AppendMode | ReadWriteMode
```

### Safe Wrapper — `withFile`

The preferred *idiomatic* form to avoid resource leaks:

```haskell
withFile :: FilePath -> IOMode -> (Handle -> IO r) -> IO r
```

> [!TIP]
> **Best practice:** Use `withFile` instead of manually `openFile`/`hClose`. The function guarantees the handle is closed even if an exception interrupts the program (similar to *RAII* in C++ or *try-with-resources* in Java).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Type / Operator | Quick Recall |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Type of main | `IO ()` | Every program returns unit |
| Sequencing (discard) | `>>` | `act1 >> act2` |
| Sequencing (bind) | `>>=` | `act1 >>= \x -> act2` |
| Get line of text | `getLine :: IO String` | Reads up to `\n` |
| Print with newline | `putStrLn :: String -> IO ()` | Adds `\n` automatically |
| Print any Show type | `print :: Show a => a -> IO ()` | `print = putStrLn . show` |
| Read whole file | `readFile :: FilePath -> IO String` | Lazy, closes automatically |
| Write whole file | `writeFile :: FilePath -> String -> IO ()` | Overwrites file |
| Safe file scope | `withFile` | Closes handle automatically |
| Interact with stdio | `interact :: (String -> String) -> IO ()` | Read stdin, transform, write stdout |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Haskell's `IO` model is the conceptual ancestor of *pure functional effects* used today in industrial settings:

- **Facebook's Haxl** — optimised I/O for spam filtering.
- **Standard Chartered's Open-Source Strats** — finance models where auditability of side-effects is critical.
- **Cryptography & smart contracts** (Plutus on Cardano) — every blockchain transition is an `IO` action, making effects explicit and reviewable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Examples & Analytical Walkthroughs

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** Each example below is *desugared* completely into pure bind-chains, so you can see the equivalence between `do`-notation and the raw monadic operators. This is a frequent KTU exam requirement.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — "What is your name?"

### Source Program

```haskell
main :: IO ()
main = do
    putStr  "Enter your name: "
    name <- getLine
    putStrLn ("Hello, " ++ name ++ "!")
```

### Step 1 — Identify Each Action and Its Type

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{putStr "Enter your name: "} &\;:\; \text{IO ()} \\
\text{getLine} &\;:\; \text{IO String} \\
\text{putStrLn (...)} &\;:\; \text{IO ()}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Desugar the `do` Block Line by Line

The first `putStr` does not bind anything, so it is sequenced with `>>`.

The second statement `name <- getLine` binds `name`, so we use `>>=`.

The third statement follows the same pattern — bind-via-lambda.

Putting it together:

```haskell
main = putStr "Enter your name: "
    >> getLine
    >>= \name -> putStrLn ("Hello, " ++ name ++ "!")
```

### Step 3 — Parenthesise Explicitly for Clarity

```haskell
main = (putStr "Enter your name: ")
    >>=
    (getLine >>= \name -> putStrLn ("Hello, " ++ name ++ "!"))
```

### Step 4 — Trace Execution (Assume user types "Anu")

1. RTS executes `putStr "Enter your name: "` → screen shows the prompt.
2. RTS executes `getLine` → reads `"Anu\n"`, strips `\n`, returns `"Anu"`.
3. The lambda receives `name = "Anu"`.
4. `putStrLn ("Hello, Anu!")` prints the greeting followed by `\n`.

**Final screen output:**
```
Enter your name: Anu
Hello, Anu!
```

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — `print` vs `putStrLn`

```haskell
main = do
    print (2 + 2)
    putStrLn (show (2 + 2))
    putStrLn "Done"
```

**Desugared form of `print (2+2)`:**

```haskell
putStrLn (show (2+2))
```

These two lines are therefore *equivalent*. KTU examiners often ask students to prove this equivalence.

### Output
```
4
4
Done
```

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — File Copy with `withFile`

### Source Program

```haskell
import System.IO

main :: IO ()
main = do
    let src = "input.txt"
        dst = "output.txt"
    contents <- readFile src
    writeFile dst contents
    putStrLn "Copy complete."
```

### Step 1 — Type Analysis

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{readFile src} &\;::\; \text{IO String} \\
\text{writeFile dst} &\;::\; \text{String} \to \text{IO ()} \\
\text{writeFile dst contents} &\;::\; \text{IO ()}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Why `readFile` and `writeFile` Are Sufficient

- `readFile` internally opens the file in `ReadMode`, reads lazily, and closes the handle when the result `String` is fully consumed.
- `writeFile` opens in `WriteMode`, writes the entire string at once, and closes the handle.
- For large files this is fine because Haskell strings are *lazy linked lists of characters*; the runtime will stream the data chunk by chunk.

### Step 3 — Manual Equivalent Using `withFile`

```haskell
import System.IO

main = do
    contents <- withFile "input.txt" ReadMode
                       (\h -> hGetContents h)
    withFile "output.txt" WriteMode
             (\h -> hPutStr h contents >> hClose h)
    putStrLn "Copy complete."
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> `hGetContents` returns a *lazy* string. You must NOT close the handle manually *before* the string is fully consumed — that is why the `withFile` idiom is preferred.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — `interact` for a Standard Unix Filter

```haskell
main :: IO ()
main = interact (map toUpper)
```

### Step 1 — Type of `interact`

```haskell
interact :: (String -> String) -> IO ()
```

### Step 2 — Execution Flow

1. Read **all** of `stdin` lazily into a `String`.
2. Apply the function `(map toUpper)` to that string.
3. Write the result to `stdout`.

### Step 3 — Test on the command line

```bash
$ echo "ktu" | runghc Main.hs
KTU
```

This single 1-line Haskell program is functionally equivalent to a complete C program involving `fgets`, `toupper`, and `fputs`.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right I/O Function

| Need | Function | Granularity | Laziness |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Read one character interactively | `getChar` | 1 char | strict |
| Read a full line | `getLine` | 1 line | strict |
| Stream all of stdin | `getContents` | whole stream | lazy |
| Read whole file | `readFile` | whole file | lazy |
| Stream one file by handle | `hGetContents` | whole file via `Handle` | lazy |
| Read a fixed number of bytes | `hGet` | N bytes | strict |
| Append to a log | `appendFile` | whole write | strict |
| Open a handle for manual control | `openFile` + `hClose` | any | depends |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of an `IO` Program (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pure Haskell source code] --> B[Type checker verifies IO purity boundary]
    B --> C[Compiled bytecode / native binary]
    C --> D[Haskell Runtime System RTS]
    D --> E[main :: IO executes]
    E --> F[Action 1 IO a]
    F --> G[Bind or sequence operator]
    G --> H[Action 2 IO b]
    H --> I[Continue chain until IO unit]
    I --> J[External world effects]
    J --> K[stdin stdout stderr files network]
    K --> L[Program exits with IO unit]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Notice the *unidirectional* arrow from `main` to the *external world*. Haskell **never** allows a pure function to "reach into" the world; effects are *one-way*, initiated by the RTS through `main`.

---

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — `getLine` then `putStrLn`

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User / World
    participant R as Haskell Runtime
    participant P as Program Action

    R->>P: Evaluate main
    activate P
    P->>P: putStr "Enter name: "
    P->>R: Render prompt to stdout
    R->>U: Display text on screen
    U->>R: Type characters and press Enter
    R->>P: getLine yields String
    P->>P: lambda name receives value
    P->>R: putStrLn greeting
    R->>U: Greeting displayed
    deactivate P
    R->>R: main returns IO unit, program exits
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Which I/O Function to Use

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Need to perform I/O?] --> Q2{Need value back?}
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{One action or many?}
    Q3 -- One --> R1[Use putStrLn or print]
    Q3 -- Many --> R2[Use do block with gt gt sequencing]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4{Reading?}
    Q4 -- One char --> R3[getChar]
    Q4 -- One line --> R4[getLine]
    Q4 -- Whole stream --> R5[getContents or interact]
    Q4 -- File --> Q5{Granular control needed?}
    Q5 -- No --> R6[readFile]
    Q5 -- Yes --> R7[openFile and hGet or hGetLine]
```

---

## 4.4 State Diagram — File Handle Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Closed
    Closed --> Open: openFile or withFile
    Open --> Reading: hGetChar, hGetLine, hGetContents
    Open --> Writing: hPutStr, hPutStrLn, hPutChar
    Open --> Seeking: hSeek
    Reading --> Open: action completes
    Writing --> Open: action completes
    Open --> Closed: hClose or withFile scope ends
    Open --> Closed: exception thrown
    Closed --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
> **What is the type of `main` in every Haskell program? Why is it not `IO Int` or `IO String`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The type of `main` is `IO ()`. The unit type `()` indicates that the program does **not** return any meaningful value to the operating system. The Haskell runtime only cares that `main` is an `IO` action; the value inside the `IO` is ignored once execution finishes. Hence `IO Int` or `IO String` would be valid only if we explicitly needed to surface a value, which is rare in top-level programs. (3 marks: 1 for type, 2 for justification.)

> **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
> **Differentiate between `putStr` and `putStrLn`.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `putStr :: String -> IO ()` outputs a string **without** appending a newline.
- `putStrLn :: String -> IO ()` outputs a string and **appends a `\n`**.
- Example: `putStr "a"; putStr "b"` prints `ab` on one line, while `putStrLn "a"; putStrLn "b"` prints `a` and `b` on separate lines. (1 + 1 + 1 marks.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**
> **(a)** Explain the concept of the `IO` type in Haskell. Why is it necessary in a pure functional language? Describe the role of the bind operator `>>=`. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write a Haskell program that reads a file `data.txt`, counts the number of lines in it, and prints the result. Use `withFile` and `hGetLine` for manual handling. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for A(a)

**Step 1 — The IO type definition (2 marks):**
`IO a` is an *abstract* type constructor parameterised by the result type `a`. A value of type `IO a` is a description of an effectful computation that yields a value of type `a` when executed.

**Step 2 — Why it is necessary (3 marks):**
A pure function cannot have side-effects. Without an explicit wrapper, a function could secretly modify a global state, violating referential transparency. By forcing all effectful code into the `IO` type, Haskell guarantees that:
- pure functions remain referentially transparent;
- side-effects are *visible* in the type signature;
- the order of effects is fixed by the sequencing operators.

**Step 3 — Bind `>>=` (2 marks):**
`>>= :: IO a -> (a -> IO b) -> IO b` executes the first action, threads the produced `a` into the lambda, and executes the resulting `IO b`. It is the primitive for sequencing dependent I/O steps.

#### Model Solution for A(b)

```haskell
import System.IO
import Control.Exception (bracket)

main :: IO ()
main = do
    let fname = "data.txt"
    count <- withFile fname ReadMode countLines
    putStrLn ("Number of lines: " ++ show count)

countLines :: Handle -> IO Int
countLines h = loop 0
  where
    loop :: Int -> IO Int
    loop n = do
        eof <- hIsEOF h
        if eof
            then return n
            else do
                _ <- hGetLine h
                loop (n + 1)
```

**Step-by-step explanation (7 marks):**
1. `import System.IO` brings in the `Handle`, `openFile`, `hIsEOF`, `hGetLine` functions. **(1 mark)**
2. `withFile` opens `data.txt` in `ReadMode` and runs `countLines`; the handle is closed automatically when the lambda finishes. **(2 marks)**
3. `countLines` keeps an accumulator `n` and a `loop` helper. **(1 mark)**
4. `hIsEOF h` checks if the handle has reached the end of the file. **(1 mark)**
5. For each non-EOF state, we read one line with `hGetLine` (discarding it) and recurse with `n+1`. **(1 mark)**
6. When EOF is reached, `return n` lifts the integer `n` into the `IO` monad, satisfying the `IO Int` type expected by `withFile`. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
> **(a)** Explain the `do` notation in Haskell I/O. How is it desugared into `>>` and `>>=`? Provide a complete desugaring example. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write a Haskell program that prompts the user for two integers, reads them, computes their sum, and writes the result to a file `sum.txt` and also prints it to the console. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for B(a)

**Step 1 — Why `do` exists (2 marks):**
Writing long chains of `>>=` and nested lambdas is unreadable. The `do` keyword provides an *imperative-looking* syntax that the compiler automatically translates (desugars) into bind chains.

**Step 2 — Desugaring rules (3 marks):**
- A statement without a bind (`p1; p2; ...`) becomes `p1 >> p2 >> ...`.
- A bind statement `x <- expr; rest` becomes `expr >>= \x -> rest`.
- A `let` statement stays in the monad's *environment* and does not introduce an `IO` layer.

**Step 3 — Worked desugaring (2 marks):**
Original:
```haskell
do
    putStr "x: "
    x <- getLine
    putStrLn ("You entered " ++ x)
```
Desugared:
```haskell
putStr "x: "
    >> (getLine >>= \x -> putStrLn ("You entered " ++ x))
```

#### Model Solution for B(b)

```haskell
main :: IO ()
main = do
    putStr  "Enter first integer: "
    s1 <- getLine
    putStr  "Enter second integer: "
    s2 <- getLine
    let n1 = read s1 :: Int
        n2 = read s2 :: Int
        result = n1 + n2
    writeFile "sum.txt" (show result ++ "\n")
    putStrLn ("Sum = " ++ show result)
```

**Step-by-step explanation (7 marks):**
1. `getLine` returns `IO String`; we bind to `s1` and `s2`. **(1 mark)**
2. `let n1 = read s1 :: Int` converts the string to an `Int` inside the `do` block (the explicit type annotation is required to guide inference). **(2 marks)**
3. The `+` operator is applied *inside* the `let`, which is a pure expression — no `IO` is involved. **(1 mark)**
4. `writeFile "sum.txt" ...` writes the textual representation of the sum to disk. **(1 mark)**
5. `putStrLn` echoes the same result to the terminal. **(1 mark)**
6. `main` ends with `IO ()` because all final actions return unit. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the explicit type in `read`:** `read s1` alone is ambiguous and the compiler rejects it. Always write `read s1 :: Int` (1–2 mark penalty).
> 2. **Confusing `do` and imperative `=` assignment:** A student may write `name = getLine` instead of `name <- getLine`. The former tries to *bind* a pure name to an `IO String`, producing a type error. (2–3 mark penalty.)
> 3. **Closing the file handle twice or forgetting to close:** Manual `openFile`/`hClose` patterns often leak handles. Always prefer `withFile` or `readFile`/`writeFile`. (1–2 mark penalty.)
> 4. **Mixing pure and impure code:** Returning an `IO` action from a function that claims to be pure (e.g. `String -> String`) is a type error. (3 mark penalty if uncaught.)
> 5. **Forgetting that `>>=` works *left-to-right*:** Students sometimes draw the data flow backward. Remember: `act1 >>= \x -> act2` reads *"do act1, feed its result to act2"*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Type of `main`:** `IO ()` — a parameter-less I/O action returning unit.
- **`IO a` is a description, not an execution.** Effects only happen when `main` runs.
- **`>>` vs `>>=`:** `>>` discards the left value; `>>=` threads it into the next action.
- **`do` notation is sugar** that desugars to `>>` and `>>=`. `x <- act` is *binding*, not assignment.
- **Standard output:** `putStr`, `putStrLn`, `print`. `print = putStrLn . show`.
- **Standard input:** `getChar`, `getLine`, `getContents`.
- **File I/O — simple:** `readFile`, `writeFile`, `appendFile`. They handle handle-lifecycle automatically.
- **File I/O — manual:** `openFile`, `hClose`, `hGetChar`, `hGetLine`, `hGetContents`, `hPutStr`, `hIsEOF`.
- **Prefer `withFile`** for exception-safe handle management.
- **`interact :: (String -> String) -> IO ()`** is the perfect one-liner for stdin-to-stdout filters.
- **No effect can escape `IO`** — `unsafePerformIO` exists but is unsafe and is *never* required in KTU syllabus questions.
- **Pattern rule:** `read` requires an explicit type annotation, e.g. `read s :: Int`.
- **Show rule:** `print` requires a `Show` instance, e.g. `print [1,2,3]` works, `print aFunction` does not.
- **Sequence rule:** Order of `IO` actions in a `do` block *is* the order of effects — reordering changes program behaviour.
- **Reference rule:** The same `IO a` value can be re-used safely; the effects are *defined* by the description, not the variable identity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reasoning about Programs
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST413 - FUNCTIONAL PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reasoning about Programs

# Reasoning about Programs — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Program reasoning** (also called *program verification* or *program logic*) is the formal mathematical process of proving that a program satisfies its specification. In the context of **Functional Programming (FP)**, reasoning about programs is performed by manipulating program text using the *meaning* of the program, treating programs as mathematical objects whose evaluation can be formally proven correct.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Reasoning about programs is the use of mathematical logic, equational reasoning, and induction to derive properties of functional programs — guaranteeing that a function behaves as intended for *all possible inputs*, not just selected test cases.

The two foundational pillars of program reasoning in Haskell are:

1. **Equational Reasoning** — substituting *equals for equals* in expressions because pure functions obey referential transparency.
2. **Induction** — proving a property holds for an *infinite* set of values (e.g., all natural numbers, all lists) by proving it for the base case and the inductive step.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Recipe Proof"**
> Imagine a chef writing a recipe for the perfect biryani. A *tester* tastes 3–4 batches and declares "this recipe works." A *reasoner* instead mathematically proves: "If you add exactly X grams of salt, simmer for Y minutes on medium flame, then the water absorption ratio will always be Z — for ANY quantity of rice." The reasoner is not satisfied with evidence from samples; they prove the property holds for *all* valid inputs using algebraic laws.

Similarly, in functional programming, a `reverse` function can be tested on `[1,2,3]`, but a *reasoner* proves the universal law:

$$ \text{reverse}(\text{xs} \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{ys}) = \text{reverse}(\text{ys}) \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{reverse}(\text{xs}) $$

This holds for *every* list `xs` and `ys` — without ever executing the program.

## 1.3 Why Reasoning Matters in Functional Programming

| Property | Imperative Programs | Pure Functional Programs |
|---|---|---|
| Reasoning tool | Complex (pointers, mutation, aliasing) | Simple (substitution, equations) |
| Referential transparency | Rarely holds | Always holds |
| Proof technique | Hoare logic, separation logic | Equational reasoning + induction |
| Side effects | Ubiquitous | Disallowed by default |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** Because Haskell programs are composed of **pure functions**, the meaning of any expression depends *only* on the meaning of its subexpressions. This makes equational reasoning valid and powerful — you can freely replace `f x` with its definition anywhere in a proof, just like substituting variables in algebra.

## 1.4 Types of Properties Proved in Module 2

1. **Equational properties** — two expressions are always equal (e.g., `map f (map g xs) = map (f.g) xs`)
2. **Termination properties** — a function always produces a result (no infinite loops)
3. **Invariant properties** — some `P(x)` is preserved across recursive calls
4. **Behavioural equivalence** — two different definitions compute the same function
5. **Total correctness** — the program *terminates* and produces the *correct* result

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Program Reasoning

### Pillar 1 — Equational Reasoning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equational Reasoning Law:** If `a = b` is a true equation in the language, then in *any* expression `E`, replacing `a` by `b` yields an expression `E'` such that `E = E'`.

**Why it works in FP:** Every well-typed Haskell expression evaluates to exactly one value (Church-Rosser / confluence property of the lambda calculus). This is the *single most important reason* functional programs are easier to reason about than imperative ones.

**How it works — Step-by-step Logic:**

- Step 1: Identify the LHS (left-hand side) of the equation to be proven.
- Step 2: Apply a defining equation (e.g., a function definition) to expand a subexpression.
- Step 3: Simplify the resulting expression using known laws (associativity, commutativity, identity, etc.).
- Step 4: Repeat until the LHS reduces to the RHS.
- Step 5: Each transformation must be justified by a known equality — *no hand-waving*.

### Pillar 2 — Mathematical (Natural Number) Induction

Used to prove a property `P(n)` for all natural numbers `n ∈ ℕ`.

**Logic Steps:**

- **Base Case:** Prove `P(0)` directly.
- **Inductive Step:** Assume `P(k)` (the *induction hypothesis*) and prove `P(k+1)`.
- **Conclusion:** By the principle of mathematical induction, `∀n ∈ ℕ, P(n)` holds.

### Pillar 3 — Structural Induction on Lists

Lists in Haskell are defined recursively:

$$ \text{List } A \;\;=\;\; [\ ] \;\;\mid\;\; (A, \text{List } A) $$

**Logic Steps:**

- **Base Case:** Prove `P([])` — the property holds for the empty list.
- **Inductive Step:** Assume `P(xs)` (induction hypothesis) and prove `P(x:xs)` for arbitrary `x` and `xs`.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Law / Theorem | Statement | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Identity for `++`** | `xs ++ [] = xs` | Tail-recursive simplifications |
| 2 | **Associativity of `++`** | `(xs ++ ys) ++ zs = xs ++ (ys ++ zs)` | Rearranging concatenations |
| 3 | **Distributivity of `map`** | `map f (xs ++ ys) = map f xs ++ map f ys` | Proving map laws |
| 4 | **Map composition** | `map f (map g xs) = map (f . g) xs` | Fusion/foldr proofs |
| 5 | **Filter laws** | `filter p (filter q xs) = filter (\\x -> p x && q x) xs` | Combining filters |
| 6 | **Concat-map fusion** | `concat (map f xs) = concatMap f xs` | Monadic-style proofs |
| 7 | **Reverse involution** | `reverse (reverse xs) = xs` | Proving palindromes |
| 8 | **Reverse-distributivity** | `reverse (xs ++ ys) = reverse ys ++ reverse xs` | Core reverse law |
| 9 | **Foldr universal property** | `foldr f v xs = f x1 (f x2 (... (f xn v)))` | Re-expressing any recursion |
| 10 | **Snoc vs Cons reversal** | `reverse xs = foldl (\\acc x -> x:acc) [] xs` | Equivalence proofs |
| 11 | **Length-concat law** | `length (xs ++ ys) = length xs + length ys` | Length lemmas |
| 12 | **All-empty law** | `all p [] = True` | Base case for `all` proofs |
| 13 | **All-cons law** | `all p (x:xs) = p x && all p xs` | Inductive step |
| 14 | **Principle of Mathematical Induction** | `P(0) ∧ (∀k, P(k) ⇒ P(k+1)) ⇒ ∀n, P(n)` | Proving number properties |
| 15 | **Principle of Structural Induction** | `P([]) ∧ (∀x,xs, P(xs) ⇒ P(x:xs)) ⇒ ∀xs, P(xs)` | Proving list properties |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Program reasoning is the foundation of *formal methods* used in safety-critical software — aviation (DO-178C), railway signalling (EN 50128), medical devices (IEC 62304), and cryptographic protocols. Facebook's *Hax*, Intel's *SPARK*, and the *Coq* proof assistant are all industrial descendants of the equational reasoning you'll learn in this module.

## 2.3 The Standard Proof Recipe for List Properties

For any list property `P(xs)`:

```
1. Case []      :   Show P([])                       (Base)
2. Case (x:xs)  :   Show P(x:xs) assuming P(xs)      (Inductive Step)
   ├── State the induction hypothesis explicitly: P(xs)
   ├── Apply the definition of the function being reasoned about
   ├── Apply induction hypothesis where applicable
   └── Simplify using algebraic laws
3. Conclude by structural induction: P(xs) for all finite lists xs
```

## 2.4 Important Reasoning Strategies

- **Generalisation:** If a property `P` cannot be proved directly, prove the stronger `P'` such that `P' ⇒ P`.
- **Fusion:** Show `foldr f e xs = g xs` by proving `f x (g xs) = g (x:xs)` — a powerful fold-equivalence technique.
- **Use of `foldr`/`foldl`:** Almost *any* recursive list function can be expressed as a fold, allowing unified proofs.
- **Lemma introduction:** Prove intermediate results ("lemmas") first, then combine.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every transition in the proofs below is **fully justified**. The text annotation after each `\end{aligned}` line explains *why* that step is valid — this is exactly how KTU board examiners expect you to write your proofs in the answer script.

## 3.1 Proof 1 — `length (xs ++ ys) = length xs + length ys`

**Recall the definitions:**

```haskell
length []      = 0
length (x:xs)  = 1 + length xs

[] ++ ys       = ys
(x:xs) ++ ys   = x : (xs ++ ys)
```

**Proof by structural induction on `xs`.**

**Case 1 — Base Case: `xs = []`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{length}([] \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{ys})
&= \text{length}(\text{ys})               && \text{by definition of } \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \\
&= 0 + \text{length}(\text{ys})            && \text{by additive identity } (0 + n = n) \\
&= \text{length}([]) + \text{length}(\text{ys}) && \text{by definition of length on } [] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** The first `=` uses the equation `[] ++ ys = ys`. The second `=` adds a leading zero, which is algebraically valid since `0` is the additive identity. The third `=` substitutes `length [] = 0` back in reverse. We have shown LHS = RHS for the base case. ✓

**Case 2 — Inductive Step: `xs = (x:xs')`, assume `length (xs' ++ ys) = length xs' + length ys`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{length}((\text{x}:\text{xs'}) \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{ys})
&= \text{length}(\text{x} : (\text{xs'} \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{ys}))   && \text{by def. of } \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \\
&= 1 + \text{length}(\text{xs'} \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{ys})            && \text{by def. of length on cons} \\
&= 1 + (\text{length}(\text{xs'}) + \text{length}(\text{ys}))           && \text{by induction hypothesis} \\
&= (1 + \text{length}(\text{xs'})) + \text{length}(\text{ys})           && \text{by associativity of } + \\
&= \text{length}(\text{x}:\text{xs'}) + \text{length}(\text{ys})        && \text{by def. of length on cons} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** Step 1 unfolds the definition of `++` for the cons case. Step 2 unfolds `length` for cons. Step 3 substitutes the induction hypothesis (I.H.), which is the *only* place the hypothesis is used — note how it reduces an unknown expression to a known one. Step 4 re-associates using `+` associativity. Step 5 folds `length` back to its cons definition.

**Conclusion:** By the principle of **structural induction**, the property `length (xs ++ ys) = length xs + length ys` holds for all finite lists `xs`, `ys`. ∎

---

## 3.2 Proof 2 — `map f (xs ++ ys) = map f xs ++ map f ys`

**Definitions:**

```haskell
map f []      = []
map f (x:xs)  = f x : map f xs
```

**Proof by structural induction on `xs`.**

**Case 1 — Base Case: `xs = []`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{map } f \;([] \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{ys})
&= \text{map } f \; \text{ys}                    && \text{by def. of } \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \\
&= [] \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{map } f \; \text{ys} && \text{by def. of } \mathbin{+\!\!\!+}, \text{ identity} \\
&= \text{map } f \; [] \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{map } f \; \text{ys} && \text{by def. of map on } [] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** The empty list as left operand of `++` vanishes (Law #1 in the formula sheet). Adding `[]` on the left of `++` also vanishes (Law #1). Finally, `map f []` is `[]` by definition. LHS = RHS. ✓

**Case 2 — Inductive Step: `xs = (x:xs')`, I.H.: `map f (xs' ++ ys) = map f xs' ++ map f ys`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{map } f \;((\text{x}:\text{xs'}) \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{ys})
&= \text{map } f \;(\text{x} : (\text{xs'} \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{ys}))   && \text{by def. of } \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \\
&= f \,\text{x} : \text{map } f \;(\text{xs'} \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{ys}) && \text{by def. of map on cons} \\
&= f \,\text{x} : (\text{map } f \; \text{xs'} \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{map } f \; \text{ys}) && \text{by I.H.} \\
&= (f \,\text{x} : \text{map } f \; \text{xs'}) \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{map } f \; \text{ys} && \text{by def. of } \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \\
&= \text{map } f \;(\text{x}:\text{xs'}) \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{map } f \; \text{ys} && \text{by def. of map on cons} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** Step 1 expands `++` for the cons case. Step 2 expands `map` for cons. Step 3 is the **single application** of the I.H., reducing the unknown subexpression to a known concatenation. Step 4 collapses the cons-on-left into a `++` by definition. Step 5 folds back to `map` on cons. The proof is complete. ∎

---

## 3.3 Proof 3 — `reverse (xs ++ ys) = reverse ys ++ reverse xs` (Lemma 1)

**Definition of `reverse`:**

```haskell
reverse []      = []
reverse (x:xs)  = reverse xs ++ [x]
```

**Proof by structural induction on `xs`.**

**Case 1 — Base Case: `xs = []`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{reverse}([] \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{ys})
&= \text{reverse}(\text{ys})                && \text{by def. of } \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \\
&= [] \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{reverse}(\text{ys}) && \text{by Law } 1 \text{ (identity for } \mathbin{+\!\!\!+}) \\
&= \text{reverse}(\text{ys}) \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{reverse}([]) && \text{by def. of reverse on } [] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** Empty list on left of `++` disappears. We prepend `[]` to the LHS (identity), then recognise both terms as reverses. ✓

**Case 2 — Inductive Step: `xs = (x:xs')`, I.H.: `reverse (xs' ++ ys) = reverse ys ++ reverse xs'`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{reverse}((\text{x}:\text{xs'}) \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{ys}) \\
&= \text{reverse}(\text{x} : (\text{xs'} \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{ys})) && \text{by def. of } \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \\
&= \text{reverse}(\text{xs'} \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{ys}) \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} [\text{x}] && \text{by def. of reverse on cons} \\
&= (\text{reverse}(\text{ys}) \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{reverse}(\text{xs'})) \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} [\text{x}] && \text{by I.H.} \\
&= \text{reverse}(\text{ys}) \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} (\text{reverse}(\text{xs'}) \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} [\text{x}]) && \text{by associativity of } \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \\
&= \text{reverse}(\text{ys}) \mathbin{+\!\!\!+} \text{reverse}(\text{x}:\text{xs'}) && \text{by def. of reverse on cons} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** The crucial step is using I.H. (Step 3) to swap the operands, then re-associating (Step 4) to re-collect them into the cons form of `reverse`. ∎

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When you see `reverse` proofs, **always** use the lemma `reverse (xs ++ ys) = reverse ys ++ reverse xs` as your workhorse — most other reverse laws are derived from it.

---

## 3.4 Proof 4 — Mathematical Induction: `sum [1..n] = n(n+1)/2`

**Recall:**

```haskell
sum [1..0] = 0
sum [1..(n+1)] = sum [1..n] + (n+1)
```

**Base Case: n = 0**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{sum}[1..0] = 0 = \frac{0 \cdot (0+1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Inductive Step: assume `sum [1..k] = k(k+1)/2`; show for `k+1`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{sum}[1..(k+1)]
&= \text{sum}[1..k] + (k+1) && \text{by def. of } [1..n] \\
&= \frac{k(k+1)}{2} + (k+1) && \text{by I.H.} \\
&= \frac{k(k+1) + 2(k+1)}{2} && \text{common denominator} \\
&= \frac{(k+1)(k+2)}{2} && \text{factor out } (k+1) \\
&= \frac{(k+1)\big((k+1)+1\big)}{2} && \text{recognise pattern} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** This is the classic Gauss summation. The factor `(k+1)` is what makes the induction "click" — algebraic factoring closes the inductive loop. ∎

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Proof Techniques by Function Type

| Function Shape | Recommended Technique | Why |
|---|---|---|
| `[]`-and-`(x:xs)` recursion | **Structural induction on lists** | Direct match with data type |
| Numeric recursion on `n-1` | **Mathematical induction on ℕ** | Mirrors decrementing structure |
| `foldr` / `foldl` defined | **Universal property of fold** | One proof structure for many |
| Two mutually recursive functions | **Induction on the larger / nested argument** | Ensure termination |
| Higher-order functions (`map`, `filter`) | **Induction on list + free variables** | Functions are first-class but inert in structure |
| Polymorphic functions | **Induction on type structure (Gentzen-style)** | Quantify universally over types |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Proof Hierarchy — Reasoning Techniques in FP

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Reasoning about Programs] --> B[Equational Reasoning]
    A --> C[Mathematical Induction]
    A --> D[Structural Induction on Lists]
    A --> E[Structural Induction on Trees]
    A --> F[Coinduction]

    B --> B1[Substitution of equals]
    B --> B2[Referential transparency]
    B --> B3[Church Rosser confluence]

    C --> C1[Base case P of 0]
    C --> C2[Inductive step P of k implies P of k+1]
    C --> C3[Natural number domain]

    D --> D1[Base case P of empty list]
    D --> D2[Inductive step P of xs implies P of x cons xs]
    D --> D3[List domain]

    E --> E1[Base case empty tree]
    E --> E2[Inductive step preserves property across subtrees]

    F --> F1[Greatest fixed points]
    F --> F2[Used for infinite structures]
```

## 4.2 Anatomy of a Structural Induction Proof

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Property to prove: P of xs]) --> Q{What is xs?}
    Q -->|xs is empty list| BC[Base Case]
    Q -->|xs is x cons xs-prime| IS[Inductive Step]

    BC --> BC1[Show P of empty list holds]
    BC1 --> BC2[Using only definitions and known laws]
    BC2 --> BC3[Result: base case complete]

    IS --> IS1[State Induction Hypothesis: P of xs-prime]
    IS1 --> IS2[Expand definitions on x cons xs-prime]
    IS2 --> IS3[Apply I.H. to xs-prime subterm]
    IS3 --> IS4[Simplify with algebraic laws]
    IS4 --> IS5[Result: inductive step complete]

    BC3 --> Conclude
    IS5 --> Conclude
    Conclude[By Structural Induction, P of xs holds for all finite lists xs] --> End([QED])
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Proof Assistant

```mermaid
graph LR
    Src[Haskell Source Program] --> Parse[Parser]
    Parse --> AST[Abstract Syntax Tree]
    AST --> Equate[Equational Rewriter]
    Equate --> Laws[Library of Algebraic Laws]
    Laws --> Equate
    Equate --> Induct[Induction Engine]
    Induct --> IH[Induction Hypothesis Manager]
    IH --> Induct
    Induct --> Tactic[Proof Tactic Selector]
    Tactic --> Cert[Proof Certificate]
    Cert --> Verif[Verifier / Kernel]
    Verif --> Out([Trusted QED])
```

## 4.4 Map of FP Reasoning Laws (Conceptual Web)

```mermaid
graph TD
    MapL1[map f xs ++ map f ys = map f of xs ++ ys] --> MapL2
    MapL2[map f of map g xs = map of f.g xs] --> MapL3
    MapL3[map id xs = xs] --> MapL4
    MapL4[map f of reverse xs = reverse of map f xs]

    Fold1[foldr f e xs ++ ys = foldr f of e ++ ys xs] --> Fold2
    Fold2[foldr op e of concat xss = foldr of op e map foldr op e xss]

    Rev1[reverse of reverse xs = xs] --> Rev2
    Rev2[reverse of xs ++ ys = reverse ys ++ reverse xs] --> Rev3
    Rev3[length of reverse xs = length xs]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Define *equational reasoning* in the context of functional programming. State one property of Haskell that makes equational reasoning valid.**

**Model Answer:**

Equational reasoning is the proof technique in which an expression is transformed step-by-step into a syntactically different but semantically equivalent expression by repeatedly replacing sub-expressions with their equal counterparts.

The property that makes it valid is **referential transparency**: a pure function `f` applied to an argument `x` always returns the same value, regardless of when, where, or how many times it is evaluated. Hence, in any context, `f x` may be substituted by its definition or result.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note (3 marks):** Award 2 marks for the definition and 1 mark for naming referential transparency (or "purity of functions / no side effects"). Writing "confluence" or "Church–Rosser" is also acceptable for the supporting property.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]`

**What is *structural induction*? State its two clauses.**

**Model Answer:**

Structural induction is a proof technique used to establish that a property `P` holds for *all* values of a recursively defined data type — typically lists or trees — by exploiting the constructors of the type.

**Two clauses:**

1. **Base Case:** Show that `P` holds for the *smallest* value of the type (e.g., `P([])` for lists, `P(Leaf)` for trees).
2. **Inductive Step:** Assume `P(xs)` (induction hypothesis) and prove that `P` holds for a value built by one of the type's constructors (e.g., show `P(x:xs)` for arbitrary `x` and `xs`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note (3 marks):** 2 marks for the definition; 1 mark for correctly listing the two clauses with appropriate list/tree examples.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each question is split into two sub-parts, **7 marks each**, with `(a)` typically being a definition / law / simple proof, and `(b)` a deeper proof or derivation.

---

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** State and explain the laws of `map` and `++` that are most commonly used in program reasoning. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Laws of `map`:**

| # | Law | Statement |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identity | `map id xs = xs` |
| 2 | Composition | `map f (map g xs) = map (f . g) xs` |
| 3 | Distributivity over `++` | `map f (xs ++ ys) = map f xs ++ map f ys` |
| 4 | Conat map fusion | `concat (map f xs) = concatMap f xs` |

**Laws of `++` (concatenation):**

| # | Law | Statement |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Left identity | `[] ++ ys = ys` |
| 2 | Right identity | `xs ++ [] = xs` |
| 3 | Associativity | `(xs ++ ys) ++ zs = xs ++ (ys ++ zs)` |

**Explanation (for marks):**
- The **identity law** for `map` reflects that mapping the identity function leaves a list unchanged — a sanity check.
- The **composition law** shows that two successive maps are equivalent to one fused map — used heavily in *deforestation* and stream fusion optimisation.
- The **distributivity** law enables us to push `map` inside or outside `++`, crucial for transforming programs (proof in §3.2 above).
- The `++` identity laws are the algebraic neutral element; **associativity** allows us to regroup concatenations freely without changing meaning.

> [!TIP]
> **Marking split:** 3 marks for the four `map` laws (with a one-line meaning); 3 marks for the three `++` laws; 1 mark for a usage example (e.g., "use composition to fuse two maps into one for performance").

---

**(b)** Using **structural induction**, prove the law:
$$ \text{filter } p \;(\text{filter } q \; \text{xs}) = \text{filter } (\lambda x.\; p \, x \,\wedge\, q \, x) \; \text{xs} $$
for all finite lists `xs`. **(7 marks)**

**Recall the definitions:**

```haskell
filter p []      = []
filter p (x:xs)  | p x       = x : filter p xs
                 | otherwise = filter p xs
```

**Proof by structural induction on `xs`.**

**Base Case: `xs = []`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{filter } p \;(\text{filter } q \; [])
&= \text{filter } p \; []      && \text{by def. of filter on } [] \\
&= []                          && \text{by def. of filter on } [] \\
&= \text{filter } (\lambda x.\, p\,x \wedge q\,x) \; [] && \text{by def. of filter on } [] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** Two consecutive applications of the empty-list case of `filter` yield `[]`. LHS = RHS. ✓

**Inductive Step: `xs = (x:xs')`, I.H.: `filter p (filter q xs') = filter (\x -> p x && q x) xs'`**

We consider two sub-cases based on the value of `q x`:

**Sub-case 1: `q x = True`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{filter } p \;(\text{filter } q \;(\text{x}:\text{xs'})) \\
&= \text{filter } p \;(\text{x} : \text{filter } q \; \text{xs'}) && \text{by def. of filter, } q\,x = \text{True} \\
&= \text{if } p\,\text{x} \text{ then } \text{x} : \text{filter } p \;(\text{filter } q \; \text{xs'}) \text{ else } \text{filter } p \;(\text{filter } q \; \text{xs'}) \\
&= \text{if } p\,\text{x} \wedge q\,\text{x} \text{ then } \text{x} : \text{filter } (\lambda x.\, p\,x \wedge q\,x)\; \text{xs'} \text{ else } \text{filter } (\lambda x.\, p\,x \wedge q\,x)\; \text{xs'} && \text{I.H.} \\
&= \text{filter } (\lambda x.\, p\,x \wedge q\,x) \; (\text{x}:\text{xs'}) && \text{by def. of filter} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sub-case 2: `q x = False`** — symmetric, dropping `x` on both sides. By similar application of the filter definition and the I.H., the equation holds.
