# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)]
**Define a Higher-Order Function in Haskell. Give one example of a built-in higher-order function and explain how it qualifies as higher-order.**

**Model Answer:**

A *Higher-Order Function* (HOF) is a function that either **accepts one or more functions as arguments** or **returns a function as its result**, or both.

The Haskell function `map` is a classic example:

```haskell
map :: (a -> b) -> [a] -> [b]
```

`map` qualifies as higher-order because its **first argument has type `(a -> b)`**, which is itself a function type. It accepts a transformation function and applies it to every element of the input list, producing a new list.

```
map (+1) [1,2,3]   ⇒  [2,3,4]
map show [1,2,3]   ⇒  ["1","2","3"]
```

Here, `(+1)` and `show` are functions being passed as arguments — confirming `map`'s higher-order nature.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]
**What is function composition in Haskell? Write the type signature of the composition operator `(.)` and demonstrate its use with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

Function composition is the operation of **chaining two functions** so that the output of the first becomes the input of the second, forming a single new function.

**Type Signature:**

```haskell
(.) :: (b -> c) -> (a -> b) -> (a -> c)
```

**Mathematical Definition:**

$$(f \circ g)(x) = f(g(x))$$

**Example:**

```haskell
negate . abs $ (-5)   ⇒  -5
```

Here, `abs` converts $-5$ to $5$, then `negate` converts $5$ to $-5$. The composition `negate . abs` is itself a function of type `(Num a, Ord a) => a -> a`, demonstrating that **the result of composition is a function** — a defining trait of higher-order functions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Following the **KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) pattern**, Module 3 questions will feature an **internal choice**. Two alternative questions are provided below. You may attempt either, but not both.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Sample Question Paper]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the concepts of `map`, `filter`, and `foldr` in Haskell as higher-order functions. Provide the type signature and definition of each, and trace the evaluation of `foldr (*) 1 [2,3,4]`.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) `map`** — Applies a unary function to every element of a list.

```haskell
map :: (a -> b) -> [a] -> [b]
map _ []     = []
map f (x:xs) = f x : map f xs
```

Higher-order because its first argument is of type $(a \to b)$, a function type.

**(ii) `filter`** — Retains only those elements satisfying a predicate.

```haskell
filter :: (a -> Bool) -> [a] -> [a]
filter _ []     = []
filter p (x:xs)
    | p x       = x : filter p xs
    | otherwise = filter p xs
```

Higher-order because it accepts a predicate function $(a \to Bool)$.

**(iii) `foldr`** — Collapses a list into a single value using a binary operator and an initial accumulator.

```haskell
foldr :: (a -> b -> b) -> b -> [a] -> b
foldr _ z []     = z
foldr f z (x:xs) = f x (foldr f z xs)
```

Higher-order because it accepts a binary combining function $(a \to b \to b)$.

**Trace of `foldr (*) 1 [2,3,4]`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
foldr \; (\times) \; 1 \; [2,3,4] &= 2 \times (foldr \; (\times) \; 1 \; [3,4]) \\
&= 2 \times (3 \times (foldr \; (\times) \; 1 \; [4])) \\
&= 2 \times (3 \times (4 \times (foldr \; (\times) \; 1 \; []))) \\
&= 2 \times (3 \times (4 \times 1)) \\
&= 2 \times (3 \times 4) \\
&= 2 \times 12 \\
&= 24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $foldr \; (\times) \; 1 \; [2,3,4] = 24$ — this is the factorial computation $4! = 24$ in disguise.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the concept of currying in Haskell. Write the standard `curry` and `uncurry` functions and demonstrate their use with a concrete example. Explain why currying is fundamental to higher-order functions.**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** Currying is the transformation of a function that takes multiple arguments **simultaneously** (as a tuple) into a **chain of functions** that each take a **single argument** and return a function awaiting the next.

**Standard Library Definition of `curry`:**

```haskell
curry :: ((a, b) -> c) -> (a -> b -> c)
curry f = \x y -> f (x, y)
```

**Standard Library Definition of `uncurry`:**

```haskell
uncurry :: (a -> b -> c) -> ((a, b) -> c)
uncurry f = \(x, y) -> f x y
```

**Demonstration:**

```haskell
addTuple :: (Int, Int) -> Int
addTuple (x, y) = x + y

add :: Int -> Int -> Int
add = curry addTuple
```

**Trace of `add 3 4`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
add \; 3 \; 4 &= (curry(addTuple)) \; 3 \; 4 \\
&= (\lambda x. \lambda y. addTuple(x, y)) \; 3 \; 4 \\
&= (\lambda y. addTuple(3, y)) \; 4 \\
&= addTuple(3, 4) \\
&= 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why Currying is Fundamental to HOFs:**

1. **Partial application becomes possible.** Calling `add 3` yields the function `\y -> 3 + y`, which is itself a higher-order result.
2. **Every multi-argument function is genuinely a function of one argument** that returns a function. This uniformity makes function composition and piping elegant.
3. **Sectioning** — a curried function can be *partially specialized*, creating specialized workers from a general blueprint (an instance of the *function-producer* HOF pattern).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Sample Question Paper]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Define function composition in Haskell. Implement the `(.)` operator and prove mathematically that composition is associative. Provide a worked example composing three functions.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Function composition creates a new function by **chaining** two or more functions so that the output of one becomes the input of the next.

**Implementation of `(.)`:**

```haskell
(.) :: (b -> c) -> (a -> b) -> (a -> c)
(.) f g = \x -> f (g x)
```

**Proof of Associativity:**

We must show that $(f \circ g) \circ h = f \circ (g \circ h)$ for all functions $f$, $g$, $h$ with compatible types.

$$
\begin{aligned}
((f \circ g) \circ h)(x) &= (f \circ g)(h(x)) \quad \text{[by definition of outer composition]} \\
&= f(g(h(x))) \quad \text{[by definition of inner composition]} \\
&= f((g \circ h)(x)) \quad \text{[by definition of composition]} \\
&= (f \circ (g \circ h))(x) \quad \text{[by definition of outer composition]}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the two expressions are equal for all $x$, composition is associative. $\blacksquare$

**Worked Example: Composing Three Functions**

Let $f(x) = x + 10$, $g(x) = x \times 2$, $h(x) = x - 3$. Compute $(f \circ g \circ h)(5)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(f \circ g \circ h)(5) &= f(g(h(5))) \\
&= f(g(5 - 3)) \\
&= f(g(2)) \\
&= f(2 \times 2) \\
&= f(4) \\
&= 4 + 10 \\
&= 14
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $(f \circ g \circ h)(5) = 14$

In Haskell, the same computation reads:

```haskell
(\x -> x + 10) . (\x -> x * 2) . (\x -> x - 3) $ 5   ⇒  14
```

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the concept of partial application in Haskell with examples. Show how partial application enables the construction of new functions from existing ones. Demonstrate with a practical example involving list processing.**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** Partial application occurs when a function that expects $n$ arguments is supplied with **fewer than $n$ arguments**. Haskell, due to currying, treats this not as an error but as a request for a **new function** that "remembers" the supplied arguments and awaits the rest.

**Basic Example:**

```haskell
add :: Int -> Int -> Int
add x y = x + y

add5 :: Int -> Int
add5 = add 5
```

Here, `add 5` is a partial application that produces the function `add5`. Subsequent evaluation:

$$add5(7) = add(5)(7) = 5 + 7 = 12$$

**Mechanism (Lambda Calculus):**

The function `add` is internally represented as $\lambda x. \lambda y. x + y$. Supplying $5$ reduces this to $\lambda y. 5 + y$, a single-argument function — a textbook higher-order function *returning* a function.

**Practical List Processing Example:**

```haskell
multiplyBy :: Int -> [Int] -> [Int]
multiplyBy n = map (* n)

*Main> multiplyBy 3 [1, 2, 3, 4]
[3, 6, 9, 12]
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. `multiplyBy 3` partially applies `n = 3`, producing the function `map (*3)`.
2. `(*3)` is itself a partially applied operator — it represents $\lambda x. x \times 3$.
3. `map (*3)` is then applied to `[1, 2, 3, 4]`, producing `[3, 6, 9, 12]`.

**Two HOFs in Action:** The expression `map (* n)` features `map` (function-consumer HOF) and `(* n)` (function-producer HOF created by partial application). This single line demonstrates the **interplay of all three HOF patterns** — a powerful showcase of functional programming's expressiveness.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Higher-Order Function Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `foldr` with `foldl`:** Students often trace both with the same parenthesization. Remember — `foldr` associates **from the right**, `foldl` **from the left**. For `(+)` they yield the same result, but for `(-)` they differ.
>    - `foldr (-) 0 [1,2,3] = 1 - (2 - (3 - 0)) = 2`
>    - `foldl (-) 0 [1,2,3] = ((0 - 1) - 2) - 3 = -6`
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Function Type in the Signature:** When asked to write the type of `map`, students frequently write `[a] -> [b]`, omitting the function argument. Always write `map :: (a -> b) -> [a] -> [b]`.
>
> 3. **Treating `curry` and `uncurry` as Inverse Operations at the Value Level:** They are inverses only at the function level, not at the value level. `curry (uncurry f) = f` holds for functions, not for arbitrary values.
>
> 4. **Missing the Sectioning Insight:** When discussing partial application, students often fail to mention that the result is a **new function** (a higher-order outcome). Examiners explicitly look for this observation.
>
> 5. **Composition Order Confusion:** The expression `f . g` means *apply g first, then f*. Writing `f(g(x))` is correct; writing `g(f(x))` is the canonical student error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Higher-Order Functions**

- ☐ A **Higher-Order Function (HOF)** either **accepts** functions as arguments or **returns** a function as a result (or both).
- ☐ HOFs are possible because functions in Haskell are **first-class citizens** — they can be named, stored, passed, and returned like any other value.
- ☐ The three foundational HOFs are **`map`**, **`filter`**, and **`foldr`** — memorize their type signatures and definitions.
- ☐ **`map` transforms every element**; **`filter` selectively retains**; **`foldr` collapses the list into one value**.
- ☐ **`curry`** converts a tuple-taking function into a curried form; **`uncurry`** does the reverse.
- ☐ **Partial application** exploits currying to produce specialized functions from general ones.
- ☐ **Function composition** `(.)` chains functions: $(f \circ g)(x) = f(g(x))$. Composition is **associative**.
- ☐ The `flip` function reorders the arguments of a binary function.
- ☐ A function of order $n$ that takes an argument of order $n-1$ has order $n$. Multi-argument functions in Haskell are technically higher-order because of currying.
- ☐ HOFs enable **abstraction over behavior**, not just data — this is the essence of functional programming's expressive power.
- ☐ In `foldr f z [x1,x2,x3]`, the operator $f$ is applied right-to-left: $f(x_1, f(x_2, f(x_3, z)))$.
- ☐ Composition reads **right-to-left in execution** but **left-to-right in source code** — a frequent source of confusion.
- ☐ The **three HOF patterns** are: **Consumer** (takes functions), **Producer** (returns functions), **Transformer** (both).
- ☐ KTU exam focus areas: definition (3 marks), `map`/`filter`/`foldr` traces (5–7 marks), currying/composition theory (5–7 marks), partial application examples (3–5 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Developing Higher-order Programs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST413 - FUNCTIONAL PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Developing Higher-order Programs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **higher-order function** is a function that satisfies *at least one* of the following two conditions:

1. It **takes one or more functions as arguments** (input parameters).
2. It **returns a function as its result** (output).

This property is enabled by Haskell's **first-class functions** feature, where functions are treated as ordinary values — they can be bound to names, passed as arguments, returned from other functions, and stored in data structures, exactly like integers or lists.

```haskell
-- Type signature convention in Haskell
higherOrder :: (a -> b) -> [a] -> [b]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:** Higher-order programming is the *cornerstone* of idiomatic Haskell. Almost every useful program (parsers, interpreters, GUI handlers, list processors) is built by composing higher-order functions. Boards routinely test `map`, `filter`, `foldr`, `foldl`, `zipWith`, and the composition operator `.`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Terminology — "Programs" vs "Functions":** The KTU module is titled *"Developing Higher-order Programs"* rather than *"Functions"*. In FP, the word *program* refers to any executable expression or collection of definitions. Since FP programs are essentially compositions of functions, "higher-order programs" simply means *programs built primarily from higher-order functions*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **factory manager** (the higher-order function). The manager does not personally assemble products. Instead, the manager:

- Hires **workers** (functions) to do specific tasks.
- Hands them **raw materials** (data — like lists or numbers).
- Either **collects the finished goods** (returns a value) or **delegates the workers to another manager** (returns a function).

A manager can be re-used with different workers — the *strategy* changes, but the *workflow* stays the same. This reusability is exactly what higher-order functions offer: **one generic workflow, many possible behaviors**.

**Real-world analogy — Food delivery app:**

| Component | Real World | Haskell Equivalent |
|-----------|-----------|---------------------|
| App workflow | `pickup → ride → deliver` | `map` (apply to every item) |
| Strategy chosen | Bike, car, or walking | Function passed in |
| Customer data | Address, item list | List argument |
| Final receipt | Delivered order | Returned list |

The workflow is the same; only the *strategy function* changes.

### 1.3 Physical / Standard Constants

There are no physical constants here, but the **standard higher-order "metrics"** in Haskell's `Prelude` (i.e., the built-in library) are:

- `map :: (a -> b) -> [a] -> [b]`
- `filter :: (a -> Bool) -> [a] -> [a]`
- `foldr :: (a -> b -> b) -> b -> [a] -> b`
- `foldl :: (b -> a -> b) -> b -> [a] -> b`
- `zipWith :: (a -> b -> c) -> [a] -> [b] -> [c]`
- `(.) :: (b -> c) -> (a -> b) -> a -> c` (composition)

These are the **bread-and-butter toolkit** of any KTU board exam answer on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Higher-order Programs

#### Pillar 1: Functions as Arguments (Most Common Pattern)

The classic *map* idea: take a function `f` and a list `[x₁, x₂, …, xₙ]`, return `[f x₁, f x₂, …, f xₙ]`.

```haskell
map :: (a -> b) -> [a] -> [b]
map f []     = []
map f (x:xs) = f x : map f xs
```

**Why this is "higher-order":** The type signature shows `map` consumes a function `(a -> b)`.

**How it works — step by step:**

- **Base case:** Empty list returns empty list (no work to do).
- **Recursive case:** Apply `f` to the head `x`, then recursively `map` over the tail `xs`, then `cons` the two results with `:`.

#### Pillar 2: Functions as Results (Currying & Partial Application)

In Haskell, *every* function officially takes exactly **one argument**. Multi-argument functions are *syntactic sugar* for chains of single-argument functions, each returning a new function.

```haskell
add :: Int -> Int -> Int
add x y = x + y
```

This is internally equivalent to:

```haskell
add :: Int -> (Int -> Int)
add x  =  \y -> x + y
```

So `add 3` is a perfectly valid value of type `Int -> Int` — a function waiting for its second argument. Passing `add 3` somewhere *is* using a function as a result.

#### Pillar 3: Function Composition (`.`)

The composition operator chains two functions so the output of one becomes the input of the next.

```haskell
(.) :: (b -> c) -> (a -> b) -> a -> c
(f . g) x  =  f (g x)
```

**Real-world engineering utility:** In compiler design, function composition models the **pipeline of optimization passes** — a constant folder feeds a dead-code eliminator feeds a register allocator. Each pass is a function, the whole pipeline is a composition.

### 2.2 The Library Workhorses (KTU Formula Sheet)

| Function | Type Signature | Behaviour Formula | Use Case |
|----------|---------------|-------------------|----------|
| `map f` | `(a → b) → [a] → [b]` | $\text{map}\,f\,[x_1,\dots,x_n] = [f\,x_1,\dots,f\,x_n]$ | Apply transformation to every element |
| `filter p` | `(a → \text{Bool}) → [a] → [a]$ | $\text{filter}\,p\,xs = [x \mid x \in xs,\, p\,x]$ | Keep elements satisfying predicate |
| `foldr (⊕) e` | `(a → b → b) → b → [a] → b$ | $f \oplus (g \oplus (h \oplus e))$ | Right-associative reduction |
| `foldl (⊕) e` | `(b → a → b) → b → [a] → b$ | $((e \oplus x_1) \oplus x_2) \oplus \dots$ | Left-associative reduction |
| `zipWith f` | `(a → b → c) → [a] → [b] → [c]$ | Pairs elements position-wise | Combine two lists |
| `(.)` | $(b → c) → (a → b) → (a → c)$ | $(f \cdot g)(x) = f(g(x))$ | Chain two functions |
| `curry` | $((a,b) → c) → (a → b → c)$ | Converts pair-taking to curried | Adapt legacy code |
| `uncurry` | $(a → b → c) → ((a,b) → c)$ | Converts curried to pair-taking | Useful for `zipWith` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall — `foldr` vs `foldl`:** Many students write the wrong formula. The `r` in `foldr` means the **operator associates from the right** (the list head is processed first), and the `l` in `foldl` means it associates from the left (the accumulator is processed first). This distinction matters for non-associative operators like subtraction.

### 2.3 Lambda Abstractions (Anonymous Functions)

`\x -> x + 1` is an anonymous function (a "lambda") that increments its argument. Lambdas are essential when the function would be used *exactly once* and naming it adds no clarity.

```haskell
map (\x -> x * 2) [1,2,3]   -- = [2,4,6]
```

### 2.4 Real-world Engineering Utilities

| Domain | Higher-order Function Used | Why |
|--------|---------------------------|-----|
| Data analysis (Pandas/SQL semantics) | `map`, `filter` | Vectorized transformations |
| Compiler passes (GHC) | Function composition | Pipeline of optimization phases |
| Reactive GUIs (Reactive Banana) | Event handlers as functions | Decouple input from behaviour |
| Neural networks (HLearn) | `foldr` over layers | Chain of layer transformations |
| MapReduce (Hadoop) | `map` + `fold` | Distributed parallel reduction |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Evaluation: `map (\x -> x*x) [1,2,3,4]`

This derivation walks through every recursive call — exactly what a KTU board examiner expects when you write a `map` evaluation on the answer script.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{map}\;(\lambda x.\, x \cdot x)\;[1,2,3,4]
&= (\lambda x.\, x \cdot x)\;1 \;:\; \text{map}\;(\lambda x.\, x \cdot x)\;[2,3,4] && \text{(uncons head, recurse on tail)} \\
&= 1 \cdot 1 \;:\; \text{map}\;(\lambda x.\, x \cdot x)\;[2,3,4] && \text{(apply } \lambda \text{ to } 1\text{)} \\
&= 1 \;:\; \text{map}\;(\lambda x.\, x \cdot x)\;[2,3,4] \\
&= 1 \;:\; (2 \cdot 2) \;:\; \text{map}\;(\lambda x.\, x \cdot x)\;[3,4] \\
&= 1 \;:\; 4 \;:\; \text{map}\;(\lambda x.\, x \cdot x)\;[3,4] \\
&= 1 \;:\; 4 \;:\; (3 \cdot 3) \;:\; \text{map}\;(\lambda x.\, x \cdot x)\;[4] \\
&= 1 \;:\; 4 \;:\; 9 \;:\; \text{map}\;(\lambda x.\, x \cdot x)\;[4] \\
&= 1 \;:\; 4 \;:\; 9 \;:\; (4 \cdot 4) \;:\; \text{map}\;(\lambda x.\, x \cdot x)\;[] \\
&= 1 \;:\; 4 \;:\; 9 \;:\; 16 \;:\; [] && \text{(base case: } \text{map}\,f\,[] = []\text{)} \\
&= [1, 4, 9, 16]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic explanation of each line:**

- Line 1 applies the second equation of the `map` definition with $x = 1$ and $xs = [2,3,4]$.
- Line 2 reduces the $\lambda$ application: $(\lambda x. x^2)\;1 = 1^2 = 1$.
- Lines 3–4 are pure structural consing — no $\lambda$ reduction.
- Lines 5–8 repeat the recursion with $x = 2, 3, 4$.
- Line 9 is the **base case** — the recursion terminates with the empty list.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Evaluation: `foldr (+) 0 [1,2,3,4]`

Recall: $\text{foldr}\;(\oplus)\;e\;[x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4] = x_1 \oplus (x_2 \oplus (x_3 \oplus (x_4 \oplus e)))$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{foldr}\;(+)\;0\;[1,2,3,4]
&= 1 \;+\; (\text{foldr}\;(+)\;0\;[2,3,4]) && \text{(unfold: } x_1 = 1\text{)} \\
&= 1 \;+\; (2 \;+\; (\text{foldr}\;(+)\;0\;[3,4])) \\
&= 1 \;+\; (2 \;+\; (3 \;+\; (\text{foldr}\;(+)\;0\;[4]))) \\
&= 1 \;+\; (2 \;+\; (3 \;+\; (4 \;+\; (\text{foldr}\;(+)\;0\;[])))) \\
&= 1 \;+\; (2 \;+\; (3 \;+\; (4 \;+\; 0))) && \text{(base case: } e = 0\text{)} \\
&= 1 \;+\; (2 \;+\; (3 \;+\; 4)) \\
&= 1 \;+\; (2 \;+\; 7) \\
&= 1 \;+\; 9 \\
&= 10
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this work even for infinite lists?** `foldr` is *lazy* — it builds a thunk. For `+`, both `foldr` and `foldl` give the same answer, but for `(:)` (list construction), `foldr` works on infinite lists while `foldl` does not. This is a **favourite KTU Part B question**.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Evaluation: `filter even [1,2,3,4,5,6]`

Recall: $\text{filter}\,p\,(x:xs) = \text{if } p\,x \text{ then } x : \text{filter}\,p\,xs \text{ else } \text{filter}\,p\,xs$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{filter}\;\text{even}\;[1,2,3,4,5,6]
&= \text{filter}\;\text{even}\;[2,3,4,5,6] && (\text{even } 1 = \text{False}) \\
&= 2 \;:\; \text{filter}\;\text{even}\;[3,4,5,6] && (\text{even } 2 = \text{True}) \\
&= 2 \;:\; \text{filter}\;\text{even}\;[4,5,6] && (\text{even } 3 = \text{False}) \\
&= 2 \;:\; 4 \;:\; \text{filter}\;\text{even}\;[5,6] && (\text{even } 4 = \text{True}) \\
&= 2 \;:\; 4 \;:\; \text{filter}\;\text{even}\;[6] && (\text{even } 5 = \text{False}) \\
&= 2 \;:\; 4 \;:\; 6 \;:\; \text{filter}\;\text{even}\;[] && (\text{even } 6 = \text{True}) \\
&= 2 \;:\; 4 \;:\; 6 \;:\; [] && \text{(base case)} \\
&= [2, 4, 6]
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Function Composition Derivation

Show that `negate . square 5  =  -(5^2) = -25`:

```haskell
negate :: Num a => a -> a
square :: Num a => a -> a
square x = x * x
```

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\text{negate} \cdot \text{square})\;5
&= \text{negate}\;(\text{square}\;5) && \text{(by defn of } (.) \text{)} \\
&= \text{negate}\;(5 \cdot 5) && \text{(apply square to 5)} \\
&= \text{negate}\;25 && \text{(arithmetic)} \\
&= -25 && \text{(apply negate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Currying Derivation: `add 3 4`

```haskell
add :: Int -> Int -> Int
add x y = x + y
```

Desugared form:
```haskell
add x = \y -> x + y
```

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{add}\;3\;4
&= (\text{add}\;3)\;4 && \text{(left-associative application)} \\
&= (\backslash y \to 3 + y)\;4 && \text{(substitute the lambda)} \\
&= 3 + 4 && \text{(beta-reduction)} \\
&= 7
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Higher-order Function

| Goal | Correct HOF | Common Mistake (loses marks) |
|------|-------------|------------------------------|
| Apply same operation to every element | `map` | Manual recursion with `(++)` |
| Keep only matching elements | `filter` | `map` with a `Bool → a` function |
| Combine two lists element-wise | `zipWith` | Nested `map` over indices |
| Reduce a list to one value | `foldr` or `foldl` | Recursion with explicit accumulator pattern |
| Chain two transformations | `(.)` | Nested calls like `f (g x)` everywhere |
| Build a new function from old | `curry` / `uncurry` | Manual lambda wrapping |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Higher-order Function Data Flow (Map Example)

This diagram shows how `map` is *parameterized* by both a function `f` and a data list, and how it produces a new list.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT FUNCTION f : a to b"] --> C["HIGHER ORDER FUNCTION map"]
    B["INPUT LIST L : a a a"] --> C
    C --> D["OUTPUT LIST L' : b b b b"]
    C -.applies f elementwise.-> D
    style C fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A fill:#A8D5BA,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#A8D5BA,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#F4A8A8,stroke:#333
```

### 4.2 Fold as a Tree Reduction

`foldr` and `foldl` differ in *how* they collapse the list — right-associative vs left-associative. The diagram below shows the call-tree structure for `foldr (+) 0 [1,2,3,4]`.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1["1 + (foldr 2 3 4)"] --> n2["2 + (foldr 3 4)"]
    n2 --> n3["3 + (foldr 4)"]
    n3 --> n4["4 + 0"]
    n4 --> base["BASE CASE: 0"]
    n3 --> res1["= 4"]
    n2 --> res2["= 7"]
    n1 --> res3["= 10"]
    style base fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style res1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style res2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style res3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Function Composition Pipeline

This is the **block-level functional architecture** for the expression `sin . negate . square` applied to input `x`.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["INPUT x"] --> S1["STAGE 1: square"]
    S1 --> S2["STAGE 2: negate"]
    S2 --> S3["STAGE 3: sin"]
    S3 --> Y["OUTPUT sin of negative x squared"]
    S1 -.returns x squared.-> S2
    S2 -.returns negative x squared.-> S3
    style S1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style S2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style S3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style X fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333
    style Y fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
```

### 4.4 The Three Patterns of Higher-order Programming

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["HIGHER ORDER FUNCTIONS"] --> B["PATTERN 1: Function as Argument"]
    A --> C["PATTERN 2: Function as Result"]
    A --> D["PATTERN 3: Function Composition"]
    B --> B1["map filter foldr zipWith"]
    C --> C1["currying partial application"]
    D --> D1["operator dot and then dollar"]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

### 4.5 Lambda Substitution Pipeline

This shows the *beta-reduction* of `(λx. x²) 5 = 25`.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["LAMBDA ABSTRACTION lambda x dot x times x"] --> A["ARGUMENT 5"]
    A --> R["BETA REDUCTION"]
    R --> O["5 times 5 = 25"]
    style P fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
    style A fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333
    style R fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    style O fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is a higher-order function in Haskell? Give one example.

**Model Answer:**

A higher-order function is a function that either **takes one or more functions as arguments** or **returns a function as its result** (or both). This is possible because Haskell treats functions as **first-class citizens** — they can be passed, returned, and stored just like ordinary values.

**Example:**

```haskell
map :: (a -> b) -> [a] -> [b]
map f []     = []
map f (x:xs) = f x : map f xs
```

Here, `map` takes the function `f` as an argument and applies it to every element of the input list. Hence `map` is a higher-order function.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Explain the function composition operator `(.)` in Haskell with its type signature.

**Model Answer:**

The function composition operator `(.)` takes two functions and produces a new function that is their sequential composition — the output of the second becomes the input of the first.

**Type signature:**

```haskell
(.) :: (b -> c) -> (a -> b) -> (a -> c)
```

**Definition:**

```haskell
(f . g) x  =  f (g x)
```

**Example:** `(negate . square) 3 = negate (square 3) = negate 9 = -9`.

The key benefit is that `(.)` lets us build *pipelines* of transformations that read like mathematics: left-to-right function flow with right-to-left reading order of the underlying data flow.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU End-Semester Examinations (ESE) for the 2024 Scheme follow a **Module Internal Choice** pattern: a single 14-mark question per module is mandatory, and the examiner provides two fully independent options. You attempt *one*. Below, both options are presented with model solutions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define higher-order functions. Explain with suitable examples how functions can be passed as arguments and returned as results in Haskell. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Haskell function `sumSquares :: [Int] -> Int` that returns the sum of squares of all elements in a list, using `map` and `foldr`. Show step-by-step evaluation for `sumSquares [1,2,3]`. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

A higher-order function is one that either:
1. Accepts functions as parameters, or
2. Returns a function as its output.

**Example 1 — Function as argument:**

```haskell
applyTwice :: (a -> a) -> a -> a
applyTwice f x = f (f x)
```

Calling `applyTwice (+3) 5` gives `(5+3)+3 = 11`. The function `(+3)` is passed as the argument `f`.

**Example 2 — Function as result (currying):**

```haskell
adder :: Int -> (Int -> Int)
adder x y = x + y
```

`adder 3` evaluates to a function of type `Int -> Int` that adds 3. So `adder` is higher-order because it returns a function.

**Example 3 — Library HOFs:** `map`, `filter`, `foldr`, `zipWith` all consume functions.

**Why this matters:** Higher-order functions enable *abstraction over behaviour*. The same loop logic can drive any transformation simply by passing a different function — analogous to the Strategy design pattern in OOP.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

```haskell
sumSquares :: [Int] -> Int
sumSquares xs = foldr (+) 0 (map (\x -> x * x) xs)
```

**Evaluation for `sumSquares [1,2,3]`:**

Step 1 — Apply the function to the list:

$$\text{sumSquares}\,[1,2,3] = \text{foldr}\,(+)\;0\;(\text{map}\;(\lambda x.\, x^2)\;[1,2,3])$$

Step 2 — Evaluate the inner `map`:

$$\text{map}\;(\lambda x.\, x^2)\;[1,2,3] = [1^2, 2^2, 3^2] = [1, 4, 9]$$

Step 3 — Now evaluate `foldr (+) 0 [1,4,9]`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{foldr}\,(+)\;0\;[1,4,9] &= 1 + (\text{foldr}\,(+)\;0\;[4,9]) \\
&= 1 + (4 + (\text{foldr}\,(+)\;0\;[9])) \\
&= 1 + (4 + (9 + (\text{foldr}\,(+)\;0\;[]))) \\
&= 1 + (4 + (9 + 0)) \\
&= 1 + (4 + 9) \\
&= 1 + 13 \\
&= 14
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** `sumSquares [1,2,3] = 14`.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the working of `foldr` and `foldl` with their type signatures. Differentiate between them using an example. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Haskell program using `zipWith` to add two lists element-wise. Show evaluation for `[1,2,3] \text{ `zipWith` } [4,5,6]`. Also write a one-line function using function composition `(.)` to compute the square of the sum of two numbers. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**`foldr` — Right fold:**

```haskell
foldr :: (a -> b -> b) -> b -> [a] -> b
foldr f e []     = e
foldr f e (x:xs) = f x (foldr f e xs)
```

Conceptually:

$$\text{foldr}\;f\;e\;[x_1,x_2,\dots,x_n] = f\,x_1\,(f\,x_2\,(\dots(f\,x_n\,e)))$$

**`foldl` — Left fold:**

```haskell
foldl :: (b -> a -> b) -> b -> [a] -> b
foldl f e []     = e
foldl f e (x:xs) = foldl f (f e x) xs
```

Conceptually:

$$\text{foldl}\;f\;e\;[x_1,x_2,\dots,x_n] = f\,(f\,(\dots f\,(f\,e\,x_1)\,x_2)\dots)\,x_n$$

**Difference example** — using subtraction:

- `foldr (-) 0 [1,2,3] = 1 - (2 - (3 - 0)) = 1 - (2 - 3) = 1 - (-1) = 2`.
- `foldl (-) 0 [1,2,3] = ((0 - 1) - 2) - 3 = (-1 - 2) - 3 = -3 - 3 = -6`.

The results differ because `-` is **not associative**. With associative operators like `+` or `*`, both give identical results.

**Lazy vs Strict:** `foldr` works on infinite lists (builds a thunk); `foldl'` (strict variant) is preferred for large finite lists to avoid thunk buildup.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Element-wise addition using `zipWith`:**

```haskell
addLists :: [Int] -> [Int] -> [Int]
addLists xs ys = zipWith (+) xs ys
```

**Evaluation for `[1,2,3] \text{ `zipWith` } [4,5,6]`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{zipWith}\,(+)\;[1,2,3]\;[4,5,6] &= (1+4) : \text{zipWith}\,(+)\;[2,3]\,[5,6] \\
&= 5 : (2+5) : \text{zipWith}\,(+)\;[3]\,[6] \\
&= 5 : 7 : (3+6) : \text{zipWith}\,(+)\;[]\;[] \\
&= 5 : 7 : 9 : [] \\
&= [5, 7, 9]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Square of the sum using function composition:**

```haskell
sqOfSum :: Int -> Int
sqOfSum = (^2) . sum . replicate 2
-- Alternatively, for two specific numbers:
sqOfSum2 :: Int -> Int -> Int
sqOfSum2 x y = (^2) (x + y)
```

A more elegant form that takes a list and returns the square of its sum:

```haskell
sqOfSumList :: [Int] -> Int
sqOfSumList = (^2) . sum
```

**Trace:** `sqOfSumList [3,4] = ((^2) . sum) [3,4] = (^2) (sum [3,4]) = (^2) 7 = 49`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `foldr` direction:** Many students write `foldr` as left-associative. The `r` stands for *right* — the operator associates from the **right end** of the list. The base case `e` is at the *innermost* position. Writing the opposite loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Type signature errors:** Writing `map :: (a -> b) -> [a] -> [b]` is mandatory. Forgetting the brackets around `[a]` and `[b]` will cost a mark.
> 3. **Mixing `map` and `filter`:** A `filter` does not *transform* elements — it *selects* them. Using `map even` and then comparing to `True/False` is wrong; use `filter even` directly.
> 4. **Skipping evaluation steps:** Board examiners award step marks for showing the **recursive unfolding** line-by-line. A single-line final answer with no intermediate steps loses up to 4 marks on a 7-mark sub-question.
> 5. **Currying explanation:** Simply stating "all functions in Haskell are curried" is *not* enough — explain *why* it makes functions higher-order (the partial application `add 3` is itself a function value).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Higher-order Programs**

- ⭐ **Definition:** A higher-order function takes a function as argument OR returns a function as result.
- ⭐ **First-class functions:** Functions are values — can be bound, passed, returned, stored.
- ⭐ **Core library HOFs to memorize:**
  - `map :: (a → b) → [a] → [b]` — transform every element.
  - `filter :: (a → Bool) → [a] → [a]` — keep matching elements.
  - `foldr :: (a → b → b) → b → [a] → b` — right-associative reduction.
  - `foldl :: (b → a → b) → b → [a] → b` — left-associative reduction.
  - `zipWith :: (a → b → c) → [a] → [b] → [c]` — pair-wise combine.
  - `(.) :: (b → c) → (a → b) → (a → c)` — composition.
- ⭐ **Currying:** Every Haskell function officially takes one argument; multi-arg syntax is sugar for nested lambdas. `add x y` ≡ `(add x) y`.
- ⭐ **Lambda syntax:** `\x -> x * x` is a one-shot anonymous function.
- ⭐ **Composition rule:** `(f . g) x = f (g x)`. Reads right-to-left at the data level, left-to-right at the pipeline level.
- ⭐ **`foldr` vs `foldl`:** Use `foldr` for lazy/infinite lists; use `foldl'` (strict) for efficient accumulation on large finite lists. They differ for non-associative operators like `-` and `/`.
- ⭐ **Higher-order programs = reusable workflows + swappable strategies.** This is the "Strategy pattern" of functional programming.
- ⭐ **Always include the type signature** in your answer — examiners give a free mark for correct signatures.
- ⭐ **Always show the recursive unfolding** for any HOF evaluation — step marks are plentiful.
- ⭐ **Real-world relevance:** Compilers, GUI event handlers, MapReduce, neural network layers, data pipelines — all built on higher-order functions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overloading, Type Classes and Type Checking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST413 - FUNCTIONAL PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Overloading, Type Classes and Type Checking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Overloading (Ad-Hoc Polymorphism)

### 📘 Formal Definition
**Overloading** in functional programming refers to the ability to assign the *same identifier* (function name or operator) to *multiple distinct implementations* that operate on different types. In Haskell, overloading is implemented through **type classes**, and is formally classified as **ad-hoc polymorphism** — a kind of polymorphism where a single name can denote a *family of functions* whose behavior is dispatched based on the **type of the arguments**, not the type parameter of a generic definition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams**
> - **Parametric Polymorphism** → One body works for *all* types (e.g., `length :: [a] -> Int`)
> - **Ad-hoc Polymorphism (Overloading)** → Different bodies for *different* types, selected by type-class resolution (e.g., `(+) :: Num a => a -> a -> a`)

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy
> Think of the English verb **"to open"**:
> - You **open** a *door* (push/pull a handle)
> - You **open** a *bank account* (fill a form)
> - You **open** a *file* (double-click)
>
> The **same word** triggers **different actions** depending on the **object's category** (its "type"). Haskell's type classes behave like a *rulebook* that says: "Any type that joins the `Num` club must define what `+` means for it." The compiler then picks the correct meaning of `+` at compile time based on the type of operands.

### 🎯 Why Overloading Matters
- **Code Reusability** → `==` works for `Int`, `Float`, `String`, and user-defined types alike.
- **Type Safety** → Resolved at *compile-time*; no runtime dispatch overhead.
- **Extensibility** → You can add *new instances* of a class without modifying existing code (the **Open–Closed Principle** in functional style).

---

## 1.2 Type Classes

### 📘 Formal Definition
A **Type Class** in Haskell is a *collection of types that share a common interface* — a set of functions (or *methods*) that every member type must implement. A type class declaration:

1. **Declares** a *name* for the interface (e.g., `Eq`, `Ord`, `Num`).
2. **Specifies** the *method signatures* (what operations must exist).
3. **Optionally** provides *default method implementations* using other methods of the same class.

When a concrete type provides implementations for all required methods, it is said to **instantiate** that class.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction**
> A type class is **NOT** a class in the OOP sense. It does **not** create objects, has **no inheritance of data**, and instances are **not subclasses**. The closest OOP analog is an **interface** in Java/C# or a **trait** in Rust, but with one critical difference: *type classes are open and retroactive* (any module can add an instance for any type, including types from other libraries).

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy
> Imagine a **professional certification**:
> - **The Certification Board** = the type class (defines the syllabus)
> - **Certified Professionals** = types that have an instance (they passed the exam by providing all required skills)
> - **Skills tested** = the methods (e.g., `Eq` requires the skill of "being equality-comparable")
>
> So `Int`, `Float`, and `Bool` are "certified members" of the `Eq` club because they each know how to answer the question "are you equal to this other value?"

### 📚 Standard Type Classes in Haskell (KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlights)

| Type Class | Key Methods | Purpose | Example Members |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Eq` | `(==)`, `(/=)` | Equality testing | `Int`, `Char`, `Bool`, `[a]` |
| `Ord` | `compare`, `(<)`, `(<=)`, `(>)`, `(>=)`, `min`, `max` | Ordering | `Int`, `Float`, `Char` |
| `Show` | `show` | Convert to `String` | Almost all types |
| `Read` | `read` | Parse from `String` | Almost all types |
| `Num` | `(+)`, `(-)`, `(*)`, `negate`, `abs`, `signum`, `fromInteger` | Numeric operations | `Int`, `Integer`, `Float`, `Double` |
| `Integral` | `div`, `mod`, `quot`, `rem` | Integer-only operations | `Int`, `Integer` |
| `Floating` | `pi`, `exp`, `log`, `sin`, `cos` | Real-valued operations | `Float`, `Double` |

---

## 1.3 Type Checking

### 📘 Formal Definition
**Type Checking** is the process of *verifying that a program is type-correct* — i.e., that every expression in the program produces a value whose type is *consistent* with the type expected by its surrounding context. In Haskell, type checking is **static** (performed at compile time, before execution), **sound** (well-typed programs cannot go wrong), and **inferential** (the compiler *deduces* types rather than requiring the programmer to annotate them).

The dominant algorithm used by GHC (and the formal reference for KTU theory) is **Algorithm W** (Milner, 1978), which extends **Hindley–Milner type inference** with type-class constraint resolution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Soundness Theorem**: If `Γ ⊢ e : τ` (a closed expression `e` is assigned type `τ` under environment `Γ`), then evaluation of `e` cannot produce a runtime type error (Hindley–Milner soundness + parametricity).

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy
> Think of **type checking** as a *strict customs officer at an international airport*:
> - Every item in your luggage (every expression) must have a **declared purpose** (a type).
> - The officer **verifies** (checks) that the purpose matches the destination's requirements (the context).
> - The officer is **smart enough** to figure out the purpose *from the contents* if you didn't declare it (type inference).
> - The officer keeps a **registry of rules** (the type-class constraints) that say "if you bring food (a type in class `Show`), you must have a label (implement `show`)."

### 🔍 Static vs Dynamic Type Checking
- **Static (Haskell, ML, Java)** → Errors caught *before* execution; faster runtime, more verbose compiler work.
- **Dynamic (Python, JavaScript)** → Errors caught *during* execution; more flexible, less safe.
- **Hybrid (C# `dynamic`, TypeScript)** → Static analysis with escape hatches.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Type Class Declaration

A type class is declared using the `class` keyword. The general syntax is:

```haskell
class (Superclass1 a, ..., Superclassk a) => ClassName a where
    method1 :: TypeSig1
    method2 :: TypeSig2
    ...
    methodN :: TypeSigN
```

### 🔬 Worked Example — The `Eq` Class

```haskell
class Eq a where
    (==) :: a -> a -> Bool
    (/=) :: a -> a -> Bool
    x /= y = not (x == y)   -- default method using ==
```

**Why each part matters:**
- `class Eq a where` → Introduces a new type class `Eq` parameterized by type variable `a`.
- `(==) :: a -> a -> Bool` → A *method signature* (not implementation) — every instance **must** define it.
- `x /= y = not (x == y)` → A *default implementation* using another method — instances **may** override, but are not *forced* to.
- The `a` is a **phantom type variable** here; it represents "the type that is joining the Eq club".

---

## 2.2 Instance Declarations

```haskell
instance Eq Bool where
    True  == True  = True
    False == False = True
    _     == _     = False
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall**: An instance declaration is *not* the same as data declaration. The instance does **not** introduce a new type; it declares that an **existing type** (here `Bool`) belongs to an **existing class** (here `Eq`) by providing all non-default methods.

### The `deriving` Clause — Automatic Instance Generation

For "boilerplate" classes like `Eq`, `Ord`, `Show`, and `Read`, Haskell can **auto-generate** instances from the structure of a data type:

```haskell
data Color = Red | Green | Blue deriving (Eq, Ord, Show, Read)
```

The compiler uses the **derived instance** algorithm, which compares/orders/prints values based on the *constructor order* of the data type.

---

## 2.3 Type Class Hierarchy (Subclassing)

A type class can extend one or more other classes via the **context arrow** `=>`:

```haskell
class (Eq a, Show a) => Num a where
    (+) :: a -> a -> a
    (-) :: a -> a -> a
    (*) :: a -> a -> a
    negate :: a -> a
    abs :: a -> a
    signum :: a -> a
    fromInteger :: Integer -> a
```

Reading this: "A type `a` can be in the class `Num` **only if** it is *already* in both `Eq` and `Show`." The full hierarchy:

```
                     Eq
                      |
                     Ord

        Eq + Show
            |
           Num
            |
        +---+---+
        |       |
     Integral  Fractional
        |
    (specialized)
```

---

## 2.4 Hindley–Milner Type Inference (Algorithm W)

The compiler performs type checking in **two broad phases**:

### Phase 1 — Constraint Generation
The expression is traversed; for each sub-expression, a type variable is introduced (e.g., `α`, `β`, `γ`) and **typing rules** generate equations between types.

### Phase 2 — Unification
The generated equations are **solved** using Robinson's **unification algorithm** (1965), which produces a **most general unifier (MGU)** — a substitution `σ` that makes both sides of every equation syntactically equal.

### Phase 3 — Class Constraint Resolution
After unification, any **type-class predicates** (e.g., `Num α`, `Eq β`) are **checked** against the global instance environment. If a required instance is missing, the compiler reports an *ambiguous type* or *no instance* error.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the **formal type-theoretic machinery** required for KTU 2024 Scheme examination answers. Memorize the *syntax* of each rule; examiners award partial credit for correctly stating the rule name even if the full derivation is incomplete.

| # | Rule / Concept | Formal Statement | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Typing Judgment** | $\Gamma \vdash e : \tau$ | Under environment $\Gamma$, expression $e$ has type $\tau$. |
| 2 | **Type Scheme (Polytype)** | $\sigma ::= \tau \;\vert\; \forall \alpha.\sigma$ | A type possibly universally quantified over variables. |
| 3 | **Generalization** | $\text{gen}(\Gamma, \tau) = \forall \bar{\alpha}.\tau$, where $\bar{\alpha} = \text{ftv}(\tau) \setminus \text{ftv}(\Gamma)$ | Quantify over type variables free in $\tau$ but not in $\Gamma$. |
| 4 | **Instantiation** | $\text{inst}(\forall \bar{\alpha}.\tau) = \tau[\bar{\alpha} \mapsto \bar{\tau}']$ for *fresh* $\bar{\tau}'$ | Replace bound variables with fresh type variables. |
| 5 | **Unification** | $\text{unify}(t_1, t_2) = \sigma$ s.t. $\sigma(t_1) \equiv \sigma(t_2)$ | Find substitution making two types syntactically equal. |
| 6 | **Occurs Check** | $\text{unify}(\alpha, \tau)$ fails if $\alpha \in \text{ftv}(\tau)$ | Prevents infinite types like $\alpha = \alpha \to \text{Int}$. |
| 7 | **Let-Polymorphism** | $\dfrac{\Gamma, x : \sigma \vdash e : \tau}{\Gamma \vdash \text{let } x = e_1 \text{ in } e_2 : \tau}$ | `let`-bindings generalize, lambda-bindings do **not**. |
| 8 | **Function Application** | $\dfrac{\Gamma \vdash e_1 : \tau_2 \to \tau \quad \Gamma \vdash e_2 : \tau_2}{\Gamma \vdash e_1\, e_2 : \tau}$ | Match argument type with function's input. |
| 9 | **Class Constraint** | $\dfrac{C\,\tau \in \text{inst-env} \quad \Gamma \vdash e : \tau}{\Gamma \vdash e : C\,\tau}$ | Verify a type-class predicate via the instance environment. |
| 10 | **Compose Substitution** | $(\sigma_2 \circ \sigma_1)(x) = \sigma_2(\sigma_1(x))$ | Apply $\sigma_1$ first, then $\sigma_2$. |

> **Engineering Utility** — These rules power every statically-typed functional compiler. The same machinery underlies ML, OCaml, F#, Elm, PureScript, and parts of Rust's trait solver.

---

## 2.6 Why Haskell Type Checking is "Best in Class"

| Property | Static Type Check | Dynamic Type Check | Haskell's HM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Catches type errors | ✅ Before run | ❌ At run | ✅ Before run |
| Requires annotations | ⚠️ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ **Infers** |
| Hindley–Milner | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Full |
| Parametric polymorphism | ⚠️ Partial | ✅ | ✅ Native |
| Ad-hoc polymorphism (type classes) | ❌ | ⚠️ Via duck-typing | ✅ First-class |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Type Inference of `(\x -> x) 5`

We will trace Algorithm W on the application of the identity function to the integer literal `5`.

### 🎯 Goal
Derive the type of the expression `((\x -> x) 5)`.

### 📥 Setup
- Initial environment: $\Gamma_0 = \{ (+) : \text{Num}\ a \Rightarrow a \to a \to a,\ 5 : \text{Num}\ a \Rightarrow a \}$
- Each sub-expression gets a **fresh type variable**:
  - Let $E_1 = \lambda x.\, x$ have type $\alpha$
  - Let $E_2 = 5$ have type $\beta$
  - Let $E_0 = E_1\,E_2$ have type $\gamma$

### 🔢 Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1** — Type the abstraction $\lambda x.\,x$.

The lambda introduces a fresh type variable $\alpha_1$ for $x$ and concludes the function maps $\alpha_1 \to \alpha_1$ (since the body has the same type as the parameter).

$$
\dfrac{\Gamma_0,\, x : \alpha_1 \vdash x : \alpha_1}{\Gamma_0 \vdash \lambda x.\,x : \alpha_1 \to \alpha_1}
$$

So $\alpha = \alpha_1 \to \alpha_1$ and the class context is empty: $\sigma_1 = \forall.\, \alpha_1 \to \alpha_1$.

**Step 2** — Type the literal `5`.

The literal `5` is *polymorphic* over the `Num` class. Using the **instantiation rule**:

$$
\text{inst}(\text{Num}\ a \Rightarrow a) = \beta_1
\quad\text{(fresh variable, with constraint Num } \beta_1\text{)}
$$

So $\beta = \beta_1$ and the constraint set is $C_2 = \{\text{Num}\ \beta_1\}$.

**Step 3** — Apply the function $E_1$ to $E_2$.

By the **function application rule**, the result type of $E_1$ (which is $\alpha_1 \to \alpha_1$) must match the argument type $\beta_1$. This produces the equation:

$$
\alpha_1 \to \alpha_1 \;\equiv\; \beta_1 \to \gamma
$$

**Step 4** — Unify $\alpha_1 \to \alpha_1$ with $\beta_1 \to \gamma$.

Unification proceeds structurally (codomain-to-codomain, domain-to-domain):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{unify}(\alpha_1 \to \alpha_1,\ \beta_1 \to \gamma) \\
&\Rightarrow \text{unify}(\alpha_1, \beta_1) \;\cup\; \text{unify}(\alpha_1, \gamma) \\
&\Rightarrow \sigma_a = [\alpha_1 \mapsto \beta_1] \;\cup\; [\alpha_1 \mapsto \gamma] \\
&\Rightarrow \sigma_b = [\alpha_1 \mapsto \beta_1,\ \gamma \mapsto \beta_1]
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply $\sigma_b$ to the overall type. The result type is $\gamma$, which under $\sigma_b$ becomes $\beta_1$.

**Step 5** — Resolve class constraints.

The accumulated constraint is $C = \{\text{Num}\ \beta_1\}$. The defaulting rule (GHC) resolves $\beta_1$ to `Integer` (or `Double` if a fractional literal is present) when no other context forces a choice.

### ✅ Final Type

$$
(\lambda x.\,x)\ 5 \;::\; \text{Integer}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Type Class Constraint Resolution for `(==)`

Trace the expression: `if (x == y) then x else y` where `x :: a`, `y :: a`.

### 🎯 Goal
Derive the type-class constraint required for the `==` operator to type-check.

### 📥 Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1** — The operator `(==)` has the class signature:

$$
(==) :: \text{Eq}\ a \Rightarrow a \to a \to \text{Bool}
$$

**Step 2** — Instantiate this scheme with a fresh variable $\alpha_2$:

$$
\text{inst}(\text{Eq}\ a \Rightarrow a \to a \to \text{Bool}) = \alpha_2 \to \alpha_2 \to \text{Bool}, \quad C_1 = \{\text{Eq}\ \alpha_2\}
$$

**Step 3** — Apply `(==)` to $x$ and $y$ with types $a$ and $a$:

- Constraint from argument 1: $a \equiv \alpha_2$
- Constraint from argument 2: $a \equiv \alpha_2$ (trivially satisfied)
- Result: `Bool`

**Step 4** — Propagate the class constraint to the enclosing `if`:

The boolean result matches the expected type of the `if`'s condition. The constraint propagates upward:

$$
\frac{\Gamma \vdash (x == y) : \text{Bool} \quad \Gamma \vdash x : a \quad \Gamma \vdash y : a}{\Gamma \vdash \text{if}\,(x == y)\,\text{then}\,x\,\text{else}\,y : a \quad \text{where} \quad \text{Eq}\ a \in C}
$$

### ✅ Final Type

The expression has type $a$ with the constraint $\text{Eq}\ a$:

$$
\text{Eq}\ a \Rightarrow a
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note**: Notice that the *body* of a `let` is checked in an *extended* environment, but the *parameter* of a lambda is **monomorphic** within the lambda body. This distinction (let-polymorphism vs. lambda-monomorphism) is the #1 source of confusion in exam answers.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Polymorphism Flavors

| Feature | Parametric (Haskell `[a]`) | Ad-hoc (Type Class `Eq a`) | Subtype (OOP `Object`) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Type variables | Class constraints | Inheritance |
| Decision time | Compile time | Compile time | Runtime (virtual dispatch) |
| New types need | No change | New `instance` declaration | New subclass |
| Errors caught | Compile time | Compile time | Runtime (sometimes) |
| Performance | Zero overhead | Zero overhead | Virtual table lookup |
| KTU 2024 weight | High | Very High | Low (contrast only) |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation of Unification — Worked Example

Unify $\tau_1 = \alpha \to \beta$ with $\tau_2 = \text{Int} \to \gamma \to \delta$.

### 📐 Algorithm

Unification works by case analysis on the *top-level type constructor* of both sides.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{unify}(\alpha \to \beta,\ \text{Int} \to \gamma \to \delta) \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Both sides are functions } (\to). \text{ Match components:} \\
                     & \quad \bullet \text{domain: } \alpha \equiv \text{Int} \quad \sigma_1 = [\alpha \mapsto \text{Int}] \\
                     & \quad \bullet \text{codomain: } \beta \equiv \gamma \to \delta \quad \sigma_2 = [\beta \mapsto \gamma \to \delta] \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Compose: } \sigma = \sigma_2 \circ \sigma_1 = [\alpha \mapsto \text{Int},\ \beta \mapsto \gamma \to \delta] \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Apply } \sigma \text{ to a fresh unifier-bound expression. Result is well-typed.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Utility** — This exact unification algorithm is what makes Haskell's compiler so fast: it runs in *almost-linear time* in the size of the program (Damas–Milner, 1982), and produces optimal types without backtracking.

---

## 3.5 Common Failure Modes in Type Checking (Examiner's Diagnostic Table)

| Symptom | Likely Cause | Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| "No instance for `Eq Foo`" | Type `Foo` not declared in `Eq` | Add `deriving Eq` or write `instance Eq Foo` |
| "Couldn't match expected type `a` with `Int`" | Type too specific in context | Add type annotation or generalize |
| "Ambiguous type variable `a0`" | No class constraint to default | Add `(Eq a, Show a) =>` or explicit `:: Int` |
| Occurs check failure | Trying to unify `a` with `[a]` | Refactor recursive type to use fixed-point newtype |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Haskell Type Class Hierarchy (Architecture Flow)

This Mermaid diagram renders the inheritance relationships among the standard Prelude type classes. It functions as a *block-level functional architecture flow* — each node is a "module" providing capabilities to its descendant "modules".

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef tier1 fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,color:#fff
    classDef tier2 fill:#10b981,stroke:#047857,color:#fff
    classDef tier3 fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#b45309,color:#fff

    A[Eq]:::root
    B[Ord]:::tier1
    C[Show]:::tier1
    D[Read]:::tier1
    E[Num]:::tier2
    F[Real]:::tier2
    G[Integral]:::tier3
    H[Fractional]:::tier3
    I[Floating]:::tier3

    A --> B
    A --> E
    A --> F
    C --> E
    F --> E
    E --> G
    E --> H
    H --> I
```

> **Reading the Diagram**: An arrow $X \to Y$ means "Class $X$ is a *superclass* of $Y$". So `Num` requires both `Eq` and `Show`; `Integral` requires `Num` (transitively `Eq` and `Show`); `Floating` requires `Fractional` which requires `Num`.

---

## 4.2 Algorithm W — Type Inference Process (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef step fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e40af,color:#fff
    classDef gate fill:#ef4444,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#fff
    classDef out fill:#22c55e,stroke:#14532d,color:#fff

    S1[Parse Source into AST]:::step
    S2[Assign Fresh Type Variable to Each Node]:::step
    S3[Apply Typing Rules to Generate Equations]:::step
    S4{Unification Phase}:::gate
    S5[Apply Occurs Check to Each Equation]:::step
    S6{Equation Solvable?}:::gate
    S7[Compute Most General Unifier MGU]:::step
    S8[Compose Substitutions Across AST]:::step
    S9[Resolve Class Constraints via Instance Env]:::step
    S10{All Constraints Satisfied?}:::gate
    S11[Type Check PASS - Emit Bytecode]:::out
    S12[Type Check FAIL - Report Error]:::out

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S6 -->|Yes| S7
    S6 -->|No| S12
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10
    S10 -->|Yes| S11
    S10 -->|No| S12
```

---

## 4.3 Type Class Mechanism — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef src fill:#6366f1,stroke:#312e81,color:#fff
    classDef cls fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#fff
    classDef ins fill:#10b981,stroke:#064e3b,color:#fff
    classDef call fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#fff
    classDef res fill:#ec4899,stroke:#831843,color:#fff

    A[User Code: x == y]:::src
    B[Compiler looks up class of operator]:::cls
    C[Class Eq - method signature]:::cls
    D[Instance Env - lookup Eq for type of x]:::ins
    E[Concrete Implementation of for that type]:::ins
    F[Compile-Time Resolution - no runtime cost]:::res
    G[Machine Code Generated]:::res

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
```

---

## 4.4 Overloading Resolution Sequence (Detailed)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Expression
    participant TC as Type Checker
    participant CS as Class Signature Table
    participant IE as Instance Environment
    participant CO as Code Generator

    U->>TC: Submit (x == y) with x : a, y : a
    TC->>CS: Query signature of ==
    CS-->>TC: Eq a => a -> a -> Bool
    TC->>TC: Instantiate a -> fresh variable alpha
    TC->>TC: Generate constraint Eq alpha
    TC->>IE: Resolve Eq alpha
    IE-->>TC: Instance found for alpha = Int (or other)
    TC->>CO: Emit call to Int-specific ==
    CO-->>U: Type-checked, compiled expression
```

# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define type class in Haskell. How does it differ from a class in object-oriented programming?**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)

A **type class** in Haskell is a *declaration of an interface* — a set of method signatures that types can choose to implement to join the class. For example, the `Eq` class declares methods `(==)` and `(/=)` for equality testing; any type for which an `instance Eq` is written becomes a member.

**Key differences from OOP classes:**

1. **No data fields** — type classes only declare method signatures, never state/fields.
2. **Retroactive extension** — any module can add an instance for any type, including types from *other* libraries (OOP subclasses must be declared in the same file/module as the parent).
3. **Open vs Closed** — Haskell type classes are *open* (extensible); OOP classes are typically *closed* to modification.
4. **No inheritance of behavior** — OOP subclasses inherit code; type-class instances must *redefine* every method (though default methods can reduce duplication).
5. **Dispatch target** — Type classes dispatch on the *type of the argument*; OOP virtual methods dispatch on the *runtime class of the receiver* (which can differ from its static type).

*(1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the comparison — at least 3 distinct differences required.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the difference between parametric polymorphism and ad-hoc polymorphism? Give one example of each from Haskell.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Aspect | Parametric Polymorphism | Ad-Hoc Polymorphism |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A single function body works uniformly for *all* types. | The same function name has *different* bodies for *different* types. |
| **Type parameter** | Universally quantified once | Type-class constrained |
| **Haskell syntax** | `id :: a -> a` | `(==) :: Eq a => a -> a -> Bool` |
| **Example 1** | `length :: [a] -> Int` | `(+) :: Num a => a -> a -> a` |
| **Example 2** | `fst :: (a, b) -> a` | `show :: Show a => a -> String` |
| **KTU keyword** | *Generics* in Java | *Overloading* in C++ |

*(1 mark each for definition + 1 example, 1 mark for the second example.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions carry **internal choice**. Both alternatives are provided below; examiners pick the higher-scoring answer.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the `Eq` type class. Write an instance of `Eq` for a user-defined `data Tree a = Empty | Node (Tree a) a (Tree a)`. Explain how the derived `Eq` would differ if you used `deriving Eq`.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain Algorithm W (Hindley–Milner type inference) with a suitable example. Show the inference steps for the expression `(\f x -> f (f x)) (\g -> g + 1) 5`.

---

#### ✅ Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Definition of Eq (2 marks):**

```haskell
class Eq a where
    (==) :: a -> a -> Bool
    (/=) :: a -> a -> Bool
    x /= y = not (x == y)
```

The `Eq` class declares two methods. `(==)` is *required*; `(/=)` has a *default* implementation in terms of `(==)`. Any type that wishes to be compared for equality must provide an `instance Eq T` that defines `(==)`.

**Manual Instance for Tree (3 marks):**

```haskell
data Tree a = Empty | Node (Tree a) a (Tree a)

instance Eq a => Eq (Tree a) where
    Empty    == Empty    = True
    Node l1 x1 r1 == Node l2 x2 r2 = (x1 == x2) && (l1 == l2) && (r1 == r2)
    _         == _         = False
```

Notice the **`Eq a => Eq (Tree a)`** context: we can only compare two `Tree a` values if we can already compare their `a`-values. The compiler will recursively demand `Eq a` evidence.

**Difference from `deriving Eq` (2 marks):**

```haskell
data Tree a = Empty | Node (Tree a) a (Tree a) deriving Eq
```

The compiler **auto-generates** an instance using *constructor-based structural equality*. For `Tree`, the derived code is essentially *the same* logic as the manual instance above (recursively compare fields, fail if shapes differ). The differences are:

| Aspect | `deriving Eq` | Manual `instance` |
|---|---|---|
| Code length | One keyword | Full case analysis |
| Customization | Not possible (generic structure) | Can implement custom logic |
| Performance | Slightly slower (uses derived generic) | Tunable |
| Extensibility | Must match constructor shape | Can compare two differently-shaped trees |

---

#### ✅ Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Algorithm W Overview (3 marks):**

Algorithm W is the procedure used by Haskell's compiler to *infer* the type of an expression without explicit annotations. It works in three stages:

1. **Constraint Generation** — Walk the AST; for each sub-expression, introduce a fresh type variable. For functions, generate the equation "expected type = inferred type".
2. **Unification** — Solve the equations using Robinson's algorithm to find the **Most General Unifier (MGU)** — a substitution that makes all equations true.
3. **Class Resolution** — For each class constraint (e.g., `Eq a`), check the instance environment. Default type-class variables if ambiguous.

**Worked Inference for `(\f x -> f (f x)) (\g -> g + 1) 5` (4 marks):**

| Sub-expression | Type | Constraints |
|---|---|---|
| `f` (param 1) | $\alpha$ | — |
| `x` (param 2) | $\beta$ | — |
| `f x` | $\alpha \to \beta$ applied to $\beta$ gives $\gamma$ | $\alpha \equiv \beta \to \gamma$ |
| `f (f x)` | $\alpha \to \gamma$ gives $\delta$ | $\alpha \equiv \gamma \to \delta$ |
| `(\f x -> ...)` | $\alpha \to \beta \to \delta$ | combine above |
| `(\g -> g + 1)` | instantiate: $(\text{Num } \eta) \Rightarrow \eta \to \eta$ | `Num η` |
| `5` | `Num θ ⇒ θ` | `Num θ` |
| Apply (λf x...) to (\g->g+1) and 5 | unify $α = η → η$, $β = θ$ | `Num η, Num θ` |
| **Final result** | `δ` (unified) | `Num θ` |

Final type: $\text{Num}\ a \Rightarrow a$ (a number).

The function `(+) :: Num a => a -> a -> a` forces `g` and `1` to share the same `Num` instance, so the result is a `Num`.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is operator overloading? Demonstrate with a complete Haskell example showing how the `(==)` operator is overloaded for `Int`, `Char`, and a user-defined `data Person = Person String Int`. Include the type-class declarations, instances, and a sample function that uses `==` polymorphically.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of the **Hindley–Milner type system** in functional programming. Discuss its key properties (soundness, completeness, principal types) and why Haskell chooses *static* type checking rather than dynamic.

---

#### ✅ Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Definition (1 mark):**
Operator overloading allows the *same operator symbol* (e.g., `==`, `+`) to perform *different operations* depending on the *types* of its operands. In Haskell, this is achieved through type classes.

**Code (5 marks):**

```haskell
-- The standard Eq class
class Eq a where
    (==) :: a -> a -> Bool
    (/=) :: a -> a -> Bool
    x /= y = not (x == y)

-- Built-in instances exist for Int and Char
-- Demoing one manually for Person:

data Person = Person String Int deriving Show

instance Eq Person where
    (Person n1 a1) == (Person n2 a2) = (n1 == n2) && (a1 == a2)

-- Polymorphic function using ==
findEqual :: Eq a => a -> [a] -> Bool
findEqual target []     = False
findEqual target (x:xs) = (target == x) || findEqual target xs
```

**Explanation (1 mark):**
The function `findEqual` is *polymorphic* over any `Eq` instance. When called with `Int`, it uses the built-in integer `==`; with `Char`, the character `==`; with `Person`, the user-defined `Person == Person` check. The compiler **statically resolves** which implementation to use based on the inferred argument type — *no runtime dispatch*, *no performance penalty*.

---

#### ✅ Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Hindley–Milner Overview (3 marks):**

Hindley–Milner (HM) is a *type system* and *inference algorithm* discovered independently by J. Roger Hindley (1969) and Robin Milner (1978). It is the theoretical foundation for type inference in Haskell, ML, OCaml, and F#. The system guarantees three cardinal properties:

1. **Soundness** — If `Γ ⊢ e : τ`, then `e` cannot produce a runtime type error.
2. **Completeness** — If a valid type exists for `e`, the algorithm will find it (no missed solutions).
3. **Principal Types** — The inferred type is the *most general* type; any other valid type is an *instance* of it.

**HM in Practice (2 marks):**

For `length :: [a] -> Int`, HM infers $\forall a.\, [a] \to \text{Int}$ — the most general type, applicable to lists of *any* element type. The programmer never writes the `a`.

**Why Static Checking? (2 marks):**

| Argument | Static (Haskell) | Dynamic (Python) |
|---|---|---|
| **Error detection** | Compile-time (caught early) | Run-time (may reach production) |
| **Documentation** | Types are machine-checked docs | None |
| **Performance** | No runtime type tags | Boxed/tagged values |
| **Refactoring safety** | Compiler flags every breakage | Manual testing required |
| **Cost** | Requires more upfront thinking | Faster prototyping |

Haskell prioritizes **correctness and performance** over **prototyping speed**, making HM the ideal choice. Pure functional programs benefit especially because the type system enforces *referential transparency* and *purity* via the type checker.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing type class with OOP class** — A type class does **NOT** create objects or allow inheritance of data. Many students write "type class is similar to abstract class" — *partial credit only*.
> 2. **Forgetting the context arrow** — When writing `instance Eq (Tree a)`, you must write `instance Eq a => Eq (Tree a)`, **not** `instance Eq (Tree a)` alone. The compiler will reject it.
> 3. **Skipping the occurs check** — When asked to "show the unification steps", examiners expect you to **explicitly state** "apply occurs check, fail because $\alpha \in \text{ftv}([\alpha])$" if applicable.
> 4. **Wrong generalization rule** — Many students incorrectly generalize lambda parameters. **The correct rule**: `let`-bindings generalize over free type variables; **lambda parameters do NOT generalize** (this is *let-polymorphism*).
> 5. **Not distinguishing `deriving` from manual instance** — Examiners ask "what is the difference?" If you say "they are the same", you lose 2 marks instantly.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These Before the Exam**

- [x] **Overloading = Ad-Hoc Polymorphism** → Same name, *different bodies* per type-class instance.
- [x] **Type Class ≠ OOP Class** → Interface only; no fields; *retroactive* extension allowed.
- [x] **Class Declaration Syntax** → `class (Supers) => ClassName a where ...`
- [x] **Instance Declaration Syntax** → `instance (Constraints) => Class Type where method = ...`
- [x] **`deriving` Keyword** → Auto-generates `Eq`, `Ord`, `Show`, `Read` from data definition.
- [x] **Type Checking is Static** → Errors caught *before* execution; *no* runtime type errors possible.
- [x] **Algorithm W** → Three phases: *constraint generation → unification → class resolution*.
- [x] **Hindley–Milner Guarantees** → Soundness, Completeness, Principal Types.
- [x] **Let-Polymorphism** → `let` introduces generalized type schemes; lambdas do **not**.
- [x] **Unification** → Finds the Most General Unifier (MGU) via Robinson's algorithm.
- [x] **Occurs Check** → Prevents infinite types; `unify(α, α→Int)` fails because α appears in the rhs.
- [x] **Num requires Eq + Show** → Any `Num` instance must *also* be `Eq` and `Show`.
- [x] **Order of Class Resolution** → GHC resolves constraints left-to-right; ambiguity arises when no class forces a choice (default to `Integer` or `Double`).
- [x] **Static > Dynamic for Functional Programming** → Enforces purity and referential transparency at the type level.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Algebraic Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST413 - FUNCTIONAL PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Algebraic Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Algebraic Data Types (ADTs)** are composite types formed by combining existing types using two fundamental algebraic operations: **sum (union / |)** and **product (tuple / ×)**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Functional Programming (PECST413), ADTs are introduced in Haskell through the `data` declaration, which allows the programmer to construct entirely new types whose values are built by applying a finite set of constructors.

Mathematically, an ADT is a type defined by a set of **constructors**, each of which may carry zero or more arguments. The type can be viewed as a recursive equation over the space of types, solved by taking the least fixed point.

Formally, for a constructor $C_i$ with arguments of types $T_{i,1}, T_{i,2}, \dots, T_{i,n_i}$:

$$T \;=\; C_1\, T_{1,1} \dots T_{1,n_1} \;\mid\; C_2\, T_{2,1} \dots T_{2,n_2} \;\mid\; \dots \;\mid\; C_k\, T_{k,1} \dots T_{k,n_k}$$

The **size** (number of distinct values) of an ADT follows the algebraic identity:

$$\vert T \vert \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{k} \prod_{j=1}^{n_i} \vert T_{i,j} \vert$$

This identity is precisely what gives ADTs their name — they behave like sums and products of cardinalities.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of ADTs as a Restaurant Menu**

Imagine walking into a restaurant. The menu offers you a choice: "Pick **one** of these categories" (Soup / Salad / Main Course / Dessert) — this is a **sum type** (disjoint choice). Within the "Main Course" category, you get **both** a protein **and** a side dish — this is a **product type** (combination of fields). An ADT is precisely this idea: a parent type that is *either* one of several distinct alternatives (sum), with each alternative possibly carrying a *bundle* of sub-values (product).

A more concrete analogy:
- **Sum Type** = A traffic light. At any instant, it is **either** Red, Yellow, or Green. Never two at once.
- **Product Type** = A date. It is a (Day, Month, Year) — you need **all three** fields together to describe a complete date.

## 1.3 Classification of ADTs in Haskell

| ADT Category | Symbolic Form | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Sum Type** (no payloads) | $T = C_1 \mid C_2 \mid \dots \mid C_k$ | `Bool = True \| False` |
| **Product Type** (one constructor) | $T = C\, T_1\, T_2 \dots T_n$ | `Pair = Pair Int Char` |
| **Mixed ADT** | Combination of sum and product | `Shape = Circle Float \| Rect Float Float` |
| **Recursive ADT** | $T$ appears in its own definition | `List a = Nil \| Cons a (List a)` |
| **Polymorphic ADT** | Contains type variables | `Maybe a = Nothing \| Just a` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module specifically expects students to define, manipulate, and pattern-match on **sum**, **product**, and **recursive** ADTs in Haskell. The `Maybe` and `Either` types are also emphasized as canonical examples of polymorphic ADTs used to model partiality and exceptions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Algebraic Interpretation of Types

The word "algebraic" in *Algebraic Data Type* does **not** refer to school algebra — it refers to the **algebra of types** discovered by the type-theorist **Robin Milner** and popularized in languages like **Haskell** and **ML**. The two operations that form this algebra are:

### 2.1.1 Sum Type (Tagged Union / Disjoint Union)

A **sum type** represents a value that is **one of several alternatives**, each tagged with a distinct constructor. The cardinality is the **sum** of the cardinalities of the variants.

$$T \;=\; C_1\, \bar{A_1} \;\mid\; C_2\, \bar{A_2} \;\mid\; \dots \;\mid\; C_k\, \bar{A_k} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \vert T \vert \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{k} \vert A_i \vert_{\text{eff}}$$

**Haskell Example:**
```haskell
data Color = Red | Green | Blue | Yellow
```
Here, $\vert \text{Color} \vert = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = \mathbf{4}$ distinct values.

### 2.1.2 Product Type (Tuple / Record)

A **product type** groups multiple values **together**. The cardinality is the **Cartesian product** of the cardinalities of its components.

$$T \;=\; C\, A_1\, A_2 \dots A_n \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \vert T \vert \;=\; \prod_{j=1}^{n} \vert A_j \vert$$

**Haskell Example:**
```haskell
data Point2D = Point2D Float Float
```
Here, $\vert \text{Point2D} \vert = \vert \text{Float} \vert \times \vert \text{Float} \vert$.

### 2.1.3 Combined ADT (Sum of Products)

The general ADT combines both operations. A classic example is a binary tree:

```haskell
data Tree a = Leaf
            | Node a (Tree a) (Tree a)
```

The cardinality of `Tree a` (for finite `a`) satisfies the equation:

$$\vert \text{Tree}\, a \vert \;=\; 1 \;+\; \vert a \vert \cdot \left(\vert \text{Tree}\, a \vert\right)^2$$

This is a **quadratic equation** in $\vert \text{Tree}\, a \vert$, demonstrating that type algebra involves polynomial equations.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Type Algebra Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Symbolic Form | Rule / Identity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sum (Union) | $A + B$ | $\vert A + B \vert = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert$ |
| 2 | Product (Pair) | $A \times B$ | $\vert A \times B \vert = \vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert$ |
| 3 | Nullary Sum (Void) | $0$ | No inhabitants, e.g. `data Void` |
| 4 | Nullary Product (Unit) | $1$ | Exactly one inhabitant, e.g. `()` |
| 5 | Maybe type | $1 + A$ | $\vert \text{Maybe}\, a \vert = 1 + \vert a \vert$ |
| 6 | Either type | $A + B$ | $\vert \text{Either}\, a\, b \vert = \vert a \vert + \vert b \vert$ |
| 7 | Pair type | $A \times B$ | $\vert (a, b) \vert = \vert a \vert \cdot \vert b \vert$ |
| 8 | List type | $\mu X.\, 1 + A \times X$ | Recursive: $\vert [A] \vert = \frac{1}{1-\vert A \vert}$ (when $\vert A \vert < 1$) |
| 9 | Binary Tree | $\mu X.\, 1 + A \times X \times X$ | Recursive fixed point |
| 10 | Natural Numbers | $\mu X.\, 1 + X$ | $\vert \mathbb{N} \vert = \infty$ |
| 11 | Identity / Distribution | $A \times (B + C) = A \times B + A \times C$ | Distributive law of types |
| 12 | Commutativity | $A + B = B + A$ | Sum is commutative |
| 13 | Associativity | $(A + B) + C = A + (B + C)$ | Sum is associative |
| 14 | Identity elements | $A + 0 = A$, $A \times 1 = A$ | Void and Unit as identities |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *product* of types (a tuple) with the *function* type (an arrow `A -> B`). They are **completely different**: $\vert A \to B \vert = \vert B \vert^{\vert A \vert}$ (exponential), whereas $\vert A \times B \vert = \vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert$ (multiplicative).

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of ADTs

| Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Compilers** | ASTs are recursively defined ADTs. |
| **Networking** | Modeling protocol states (Connected, Disconnected, Authenticated). |
| **Embedded Systems** | `Maybe Sensor` cleanly models sensor availability. |
| **Databases** | `Either Error Record` models the result of a query. |
| **Cryptography** | Recursive ADTs model structured key hierarchies. |
| **Static Analysis** | Exhaustiveness checking guarantees all cases handled. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Industry:** Modern languages like **Rust** (`enum`), **Swift** (`enum` with associated values), and **TypeScript** (discriminated unions) have *adopted* ADTs explicitly — proving that this "academic" concept is the backbone of type-safe industry software.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Cardinality of `Maybe a`
